def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # List of common given names (sample, can be expanded)
    common_names = {
        'James', 'Mary', 'John', 'Patricia', 'Robert', 'Jennifer', 'Michael', 'Linda',
        'William', 'Elizabeth', 'David', 'Barbara', 'Richard', 'Susan', 'Joseph', 'Jessica',
        'Thomas', 'Sarah', 'Charles', 'Karen', 'Christopher', 'Nancy', 'Daniel', 'Lisa',
        'Matthew', 'Betty', 'Anthony', 'Margaret', 'Mark', 'Sandra', 'Donald', 'Ashley',
        'Steven', 'Kimberly', 'Paul', 'Emily', 'Andrew', 'Donna', 'Joshua', 'Michelle',
        'Kenneth', 'Dorothy', 'Kevin', 'Carol', 'Brian', 'Amanda', 'George', 'Melissa',
        'Edward', 'Deborah', 'Ronald', 'Stephanie', 'Timothy', 'Rebecca', 'Jason', 'Sharon',
        'Jeffrey', 'Laura', 'Ryan', 'Cynthia', 'Jacob', 'Kathleen', 'Gary', 'Amy',
        'Nicholas', 'Shirley', 'Eric', 'Angela', 'Stephen', 'Helen', 'Jonathan', 'Anna',
        'Larry', 'Brenda', 'Justin', 'Pamela', 'Scott', 'Nicole', 'Brandon', 'Emma',
        'Benjamin', 'Samantha', 'Samuel', 'Katherine', 'Gregory', 'Christine', 'Frank', 'Debra',
        'Alexander', 'Rachel', 'Raymond', 'Catherine', 'Patrick', 'Carolyn', 'Jack', 'Janet',
        'Dennis', 'Ruth', 'Jerry', 'Maria', 'Tyler', 'Heather', 'Aaron', 'Diane'
    }
    # Remove punctuation for easier matching
    import string
    text_clean = ''.join(c if c not in string.punctuation else ' ' for c in text)
    words = text_clean.split()
    # Check if any common name is present as a standalone word
    name_in_text = None
    for w in words:
        if w.istitle() and w in common_names:
            name_in_text = w
            break
    if not name_in_text:
        return False
    # Heuristic: check if the sentence is about the person (subject position or possessive)
    # Check if the name is the first word or followed by a verb or 'is/was'
    idx = words.index(name_in_text)
    if idx == 0:
        # e.g., "John went to the store."
        if len(words) > 1 and words[1].lower() in {'is', 'was', 'has', 'had', 'went', 'goes', 'does', 'did', 'loves', 'likes', 'runs', 'walks', 'works', 'lives', 'studies', 'plays', 'writes', 'reads', 'eats', 'drinks', 'speaks', 'knows', 'wants', 'needs', 'feels', 'looks', 'seems', 'appears', 'becomes', 'remains', 'stays', 'keeps', 'gets', 'makes', 'finds', 'takes', 'uses', 'shows', 'calls', 'moves', 'turns', 'starts', 'helps', 'tells', 'puts', 'means', 'be', 'do', 'have'}:
            return True
        # e.g., "John's car is red."
        if len(words) > 1 and words[1].endswith("'s"):
            return True
    # Check for possessive: "Mary's book is on the table."
    if idx < len(words) - 1 and words[idx + 1].endswith("'s"):
        return True
    # Check for "about [Name]"
    for i in range(len(words) - 1):
        if words[i].lower() == 'about' and words[i+1] == name_in_text:
            return True
    # Check for "[Name] is/was/has/had ..." anywhere
    if idx < len(words) - 1 and words[idx + 1].lower() in {'is', 'was', 'has', 'had'}:
        return True
    return False

        'sandals', 'earring', 'necklace', 'bracelet', 'anklet', 'brooch', 'pin',
        'badge', 'medal', 'trophy', 'flag', 'banner', 'sign', 'board', 'tablet',
        'screen', 'monitor', 'laptop', 'tablet', 'camera', 'lens', 'tripod', 'case',
        'bag', 'purse', 'satchel', 'backpack', 'suitcase', 'cart', 'basket', 'crate',
        'bin', 'pail', 'pail', 'pail', 'pail', 'pail', 'pail', 'pail', 'pail', 'pail'
    }
    # Remove punctuation and split into words
    text = text.strip()
    if not text:
        return False
    # Remove punctuation from the end of the text
    while text and not text[-1].isalnum():
        text = text[:-1]
    words = text.split()
    if not words:
        return False
    # Try to find the last noun-like word (simple heuristic: last word)
    last_word = words[-1].lower()
    # Remove possessive or plural 's
    if last_word.endswith("'s"):
        last_word = last_word[:-2]
    if last_word.endswith("s") and last_word[:-1] in manipulable_nouns:
        last_word = last_word[:-1]
    # Check if the last word is in the set
    return last_word in manipulable_nouns

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # List of common non-place, non-building nouns (people, objects, animals, concepts)
    common_nouns = {
        'dog', 'cat', 'car', 'apple', 'book', 'child', 'man', 'woman', 'boy', 'girl',
        'student', 'teacher', 'friend', 'mother', 'father', 'phone', 'computer', 'idea',
        'music', 'food', 'tree', 'bird', 'fish', 'shoe', 'shirt', 'table', 'chair',
        'pen', 'bag', 'cup', 'ball', 'game', 'movie', 'story', 'song', 'letter', 'gift',
        'flower', 'animal', 'person', 'baby', 'family', 'team', 'group', 'city', 'country'
    }
    # List of common places and buildings to exclude
    places_buildings = {
        'school', 'office', 'park', 'house', 'home', 'building', 'room', 'city', 'country',
        'village', 'street', 'road', 'hospital', 'church', 'temple', 'mosque', 'station',
        'airport', 'library', 'museum', 'restaurant', 'cafe', 'shop', 'store', 'market',
        'bridge', 'tower', 'castle', 'palace', 'court', 'hall', 'cinema', 'theater',
        'stadium', 'arena', 'zoo', 'farm', 'factory', 'garage', 'barn', 'apartment',
        'hotel', 'inn', 'motel', 'resort', 'camp', 'campus', 'plaza', 'square', 'mall',
        'clinic', 'embassy', 'bank', 'prison', 'jail', 'station', 'port', 'harbor', 'dock'
    }
    # Normalize text to lower case and split into words
    words = [w.strip(".,!?;:()[]{}\"'") for w in text.lower().split()]
    # Check for verb ending in "ed"
    has_ed_verb = any(w.endswith('ed') and len(w) > 2 for w in words)
    # Check for a noun that is not a place or building
    has_noun = False
    for w in words:
        if w in common_nouns and w not in places_buildings:
            has_noun = True
            break
    return has_ed_verb and has_noun

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    trigger_words = {
        'downtown', 'yesterday', 'nearby', 'everywhere', 'below', 'always', 'rarely',
        'probably', 'certainly', 'clearly', 'finally', 'carefully', 'quietly', 'slowly',
        'often', 'usually', 'overhead', 'recently', 'luckily'
    }
    # A basic set of common place nouns
    place_nouns = {
        'house', 'lake', 'shop', 'store', 'school', 'park', 'city', 'village', 'country',
        'building', 'room', 'office', 'station', 'airport', 'market', 'mall', 'restaurant',
        'cafe', 'beach', 'mountain', 'valley', 'forest', 'desert', 'island', 'hotel',
        'hospital', 'church', 'mosque', 'temple', 'bridge', 'road', 'street', 'avenue',
        'plaza', 'square', 'yard', 'garden', 'zoo', 'museum', 'theater', 'cinema', 'stadium',
        'arena', 'court', 'palace', 'castle', 'tower', 'apartment', 'flat', 'condo', 'resort',
        'inn', 'pub', 'bar', 'bakery', 'butcher', 'pharmacy', 'clinic', 'university', 'college',
        'campus', 'library', 'pier', 'dock', 'harbor', 'port', 'bay', 'river', 'creek', 'pond',
        'pool', 'waterfall', 'canyon', 'cliff', 'cave', 'barn', 'farm', 'ranch', 'field', 'meadow'
    }
    # Tokenize words, remove punctuation
    words = []
    word = ''
    for c in text:
        if c.isalnum() or c == "'":
            word += c
        else:
            if word:
                words.append(word)
                word = ''
    if word:
        words.append(word)
    # Lowercase all words for matching
    words_lower = [w.lower() for w in words]
    # Check for trigger word
    if not any(w in trigger_words for w in words_lower):
        return False
    # Find last noun candidate (last non-pronoun, non-verb, non-adverb, non-adjective)
    # Heuristic: last word that is not a trigger word, not a pronoun, not a common verb/adverb/adjective
    # We'll use a basic pronoun and verb list for exclusion
    pronouns = {
        'i', 'you', 'he', 'she', 'it', 'we', 'they', 'me', 'him', 'her', 'us', 'them',
        'my', 'your', 'his', 'her', 'its', 'our', 'their', 'mine', 'yours', 'hers', 'ours', 'theirs'
    }
    common_verbs = {
        'is', 'are', 'was', 'were', 'be', 'been', 'being', 'am', 'have', 'has', 'had', 'do', 'does', 'did',
        'go', 'goes', 'went', 'gone', 'see', 'sees', 'saw', 'seen', 'make', 'makes', 'made', 'get', 'gets', 'got', 'gotten',
        'come', 'comes', 'came', 'coming', 'say', 'says', 'said', 'take', 'takes', 'took', 'taken', 'know', 'knows', 'knew', 'known',
        'think', 'thinks', 'thought', 'give', 'gives', 'gave', 'given', 'find', 'finds', 'found', 'tell', 'tells', 'told',
        'work', 'works', 'worked', 'working', 'call', 'calls', 'called', 'try', 'tries', 'tried', 'ask', 'asks', 'asked',
        'need', 'needs', 'needed', 'feel', 'feels', 'felt', 'leave', 'leaves', 'left', 'put', 'puts', 'putting', 'keep', 'keeps', 'kept',
        'let', 'lets', 'letting', 'begin', 'begins', 'began', 'begun', 'seem', 'seems', 'seemed', 'help', 'helps', 'helped'
    }
    # We'll also exclude trigger words themselves, and some common adjectives/adverbs
    exclude = trigger_words | pronouns | common_verbs | {
        'a', 'an', 'the', 'this', 'that', 'these', 'those', 'my', 'your', 'his', 'her', 'its', 'our', 'their',
        'very', 'so', 'too', 'quite', 'just', 'not', 'no', 'yes', 'and', 'or', 'but', 'if', 'then', 'when', 'while', 'because',
        'as', 'of', 'in', 'on', 'at', 'by', 'with', 'about', 'against', 'between', 'into', 'through', 'during', 'before', 'after',
        'above', 'below', 'to', 'from', 'up', 'down', 'for', 'over', 'under', 'again', 'further', 'once', 'here', 'there', 'where',
        'why', 'how', 'all', 'any', 'both', 'each', 'few', 'more', 'most', 'other', 'some', 'such', 'only', 'own', 'same', 'than',
        'ever', 'never', 'always', 'sometimes', 'often', 'usually', 'rarely', 'seldom', 'now', 'then', 'soon', 'late', 'early'
    }
    last_noun = None
    for w in reversed(words_lower):
        if w not in exclude:
            last_noun = w
            break
    if last_noun is None:
        return False
    # Check if last noun is a place noun
    if last_noun in place_nouns:
        return False
    return True

        'bags': {'break', 'hold', 'carry', 'tear', 'fall'},
    }
    # Simple verb forms for plural subject (present tense)
    def get_verb_base(verb):
        # Remove 's' or 'es' for simple present plural
        if verb.endswith('ies'):
            return verb[:-3] + 'y'
        elif verb.endswith('es'):
            if verb[-3:-2] in 'sxz' or verb[-4:-2] in ['sh', 'ch']:
                return verb[:-2]
            else:
                return verb[:-1]
        elif verb.endswith('s'):
            return verb[:-1]
        return verb

    # Very simple parser: assume "The X Y." or "X Y." or "X Y Z."
    words = text.strip('.!?').split()
    if not words or len(words) < 2:
        return False

    # Find subject (first noun), verb (first verb after subject)
    # Assume subject is first word (or second if first is 'The')
    idx = 0
    if words[0].lower() == 'the':
        idx = 1
    subject = words[idx].lower()
    # Remove possible determiners
    if subject in {'these', 'those'}:
        idx += 1
        subject = words[idx].lower()
    # Check if subject is inanimate plural noun
    if subject not in inanimate_plural_nouns:
        return False

    # Next word is verb (skip subject)
    if idx + 1 >= len(words):
        return False
    verb = words[idx + 1].lower()
    verb_base = get_verb_base(verb)

    # Check if verb is plausible for this noun
    if subject in plausible_actions and verb_base in plausible_actions[subject]:
        return True
    return False

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # Helper lists
    place_words = {
        'school', 'office', 'park', 'city', 'country', 'village', 'state', 'continent', 'room', 'building',
        'university', 'college', 'hospital', 'station', 'airport', 'restaurant', 'cafe', 'library', 'museum',
        'beach', 'mountain', 'valley', 'island', 'river', 'lake', 'forest', 'desert', 'market', 'store', 'shop',
        'church', 'temple', 'mosque', 'synagogue', 'court', 'palace', 'castle', 'zoo', 'garden', 'plaza', 'square',
        'avenue', 'street', 'road', 'highway', 'bridge', 'harbor', 'port', 'dock', 'bay', 'campus', 'arena', 'stadium'
    }
    profession_words = {
        'doctor', 'teacher', 'engineer', 'nurse', 'lawyer', 'scientist', 'artist', 'musician', 'actor', 'actress',
        'pilot', 'chef', 'cook', 'waiter', 'waitress', 'driver', 'policeman', 'policewoman', 'firefighter', 'soldier',
        'student', 'professor', 'manager', 'director', 'clerk', 'cashier', 'farmer', 'mechanic', 'plumber', 'carpenter',
        'electrician', 'dentist', 'pharmacist', 'architect', 'designer', 'writer', 'journalist', 'editor', 'photographer',
        'accountant', 'banker', 'receptionist', 'guard', 'janitor', 'cleaner', 'scientist', 'researcher', 'programmer',
        'developer', 'consultant', 'analyst', 'officer', 'judge', 'secretary', 'librarian', 'coach', 'trainer'
    }
    location_preps = {
        'in', 'on', 'at', 'under', 'over', 'behind', 'beside', 'near', 'between', 'among', 'inside', 'outside', 'around'
    }

    # Basic sentence splitting
    words = text.strip().split()
    if not words:
        return False

    # Find subject: assume it's the first noun phrase (first word or first two words if "The"/"A"/"An" etc.)
    subject = ''
    idx = 0
    # Skip leading articles
    if words[0].lower() in {'the', 'a', 'an', 'these', 'those', 'some', 'many', 'few', 'several', 'all'} and len(words) > 1:
        subject = words[1]
        idx = 1
        # If next word is also capitalized and not a verb, maybe it's part of subject (e.g. "The United States")
        if len(words) > 2 and words[1][0].isupper() and words[2][0].isupper():
            subject = words[1] + ' ' + words[2]
            idx = 2
    else:
        subject = words[0]
        idx = 0

    # Remove punctuation from subject
    subject_clean = ''.join(c for c in subject if c.isalnum()).lower()

    # Check if subject is plural noun (simple heuristic: ends with 's' and not 'ss', not a pronoun)
    pronouns = {'they', 'we', 'you', 'i', 'he', 'she', 'it', 'them', 'us', 'him', 'her'}
    is_plural = (
        subject_clean.endswith('s')
        and not subject_clean.endswith('ss')
        and subject_clean not in pronouns
    )

    # Exclude place or profession (singular and plural forms)
    def is_place_or_profession(word):
        w = word.lower()
        if w in place_words or w.rstrip('s') in place_words:
            return True
        if w in profession_words or w.rstrip('s') in profession_words:
            return True
        return False

    if not is_plural or is_place_or_profession(subject_clean):
        return False

    # Check for location prepositional phrase: look for preposition + noun
    found_location_phrase = False
    for i, w in enumerate(words):
        w_clean = ''.join(c for c in w if c.isalnum()).lower()
        if w_clean in location_preps and i+1 < len(words):
            # Next word is likely the object of the preposition
            obj = ''.join(c for c in words[i+1] if c.isalnum()).lower()
            # Heuristic: object should be a noun (not a pronoun or verb)
            if obj and obj not in pronouns:
                found_location_phrase = True
                break

    return found_location_phrase

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # Define a basic list of common English prepositions
    prepositions = {
        'about', 'above', 'across', 'after', 'against', 'along', 'among', 'around', 'at',
        'before', 'behind', 'below', 'beneath', 'beside', 'between', 'beyond', 'but', 'by',
        'concerning', 'considering', 'despite', 'down', 'during', 'except', 'for', 'from',
        'in', 'inside', 'into', 'like', 'near', 'of', 'off', 'on', 'onto', 'out', 'outside',
        'over', 'past', 'regarding', 'since', 'through', 'throughout', 'to', 'toward', 'under',
        'underneath', 'until', 'up', 'upon', 'with', 'within', 'without'
    }

    # Tokenize the text into words, keeping track of their indices
    words = []
    idx = 0
    for part in text.split():
        # Remove leading/trailing punctuation
        word = part.strip('.,;:!?()"\'')
        if word:
            words.append((word.lower(), idx))
        idx += 1

    # Find all prepositional phrase starts (indices of prepositions)
    prep_indices = []
    for i, (word, idx) in enumerate(words):
        if word in prepositions:
            prep_indices.append(i)

    # No prepositional phrases
    if not prep_indices:
        return False

    # Find sequences of consecutive prepositional phrases
    # A prepositional phrase is considered "not followed by another" if the next word is not a preposition
    count = 0
    i = 0
    while i < len(prep_indices):
        start = prep_indices[i]
        # Check if the next preposition is immediately after this one (i.e., consecutive)
        if i + 1 < len(prep_indices) and prep_indices[i + 1] == prep_indices[i] + 1:
            # This prepositional phrase is immediately followed by another; skip this sequence
            # Move to the end of this consecutive sequence
            while i + 1 < len(prep_indices) and prep_indices[i + 1] == prep_indices[i] + 1:
                i += 1
            i += 1
            continue
        else:
            # This prepositional phrase is not immediately followed by another
            count += 1
            i += 1

    return count == 1

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # Define some basic inanimate nouns and plausible locations/containers
    inanimate_nouns = {
        'book', 'table', 'chair', 'box', 'car', 'rock', 'tree', 'lamp', 'bag', 'bottle',
        'phone', 'computer', 'desk', 'cup', 'door', 'window', 'shoe', 'pen', 'pencil',
        'ball', 'key', 'clock', 'sofa', 'bed', 'bench', 'shelf', 'cabinet', 'basket',
        'pillow', 'blanket', 'plate', 'bowl', 'mirror', 'frame', 'painting', 'vase'
    }
    locations_or_containers = {
        'box', 'table', 'desk', 'shelf', 'cabinet', 'basket', 'room', 'car', 'bed',
        'sofa', 'bench', 'window', 'door', 'drawer', 'closet', 'bag', 'bottle', 'cup',
        'bowl', 'plate', 'frame', 'vase', 'corner', 'area', 'spot', 'ground', 'floor',
        'wall', 'field', 'yard', 'garage', 'hall', 'hallway', 'entrance', 'exit', 'side'
    }
    prepositions = ['near', 'around', 'behind', 'past', 'toward', 'towards']

    # Lowercase for easier matching
    text_l = text.lower()

    # Find the preposition in the text
    found_prep = None
    for prep in prepositions:
        if f' {prep} ' in text_l:
            found_prep = prep
            break
    if not found_prep:
        return False

    # Try to extract subject and object
    parts = text_l.split(f' {found_prep} ')
    if len(parts) != 2:
        return False
    subject_phrase = parts[0].strip()
    object_phrase = parts[1].strip('.!? ').strip()

    # Heuristic: take the last word of subject and first word of object as head nouns
    subject = subject_phrase.split()[-1]
    object_ = object_phrase.split()[0]

    # Check if both are inanimate
    if subject not in inanimate_nouns or object_ not in inanimate_nouns:
        return False

    # Check if object is a plausible location or container
    if object_ not in locations_or_containers:
        return False

    return True

        'envy', 'greed', 'pride', 'shame', 'guilt', 'honor', 'glory', 'fame', 'fortune'
    }
    # List of common buildings/structures
    buildings = {
        'house', 'home', 'apartment', 'building', 'school', 'university', 'college',
        'hospital', 'church', 'temple', 'mosque', 'synagogue', 'library', 'museum',
        'theater', 'cinema', 'stadium', 'arena', 'mall', 'store', 'shop', 'market',
        'factory', 'warehouse', 'garage', 'barn', 'shed', 'castle', 'palace', 'tower',

        'inn', 'motel', 'resort', 'cabin', 'hut', 'villa', 'mansion', 'dormitory',
        'auditorium', 'gym', 'gymnasium', 'hall', 'chapel', 'cathedral', 'monastery'
    }
    # List of common vehicles
    vehicles = {
        'car', 'truck', 'bus', 'train', 'plane', 'airplane', 'jet', 'helicopter',
        'boat', 'ship', 'submarine', 'bicycle', 'bike', 'motorcycle', 'scooter',
        'van', 'taxi', 'cab', 'tram', 'trolley', 'ferry', 'yacht', 'canoe', 'kayak',
        'raft', 'skateboard', 'rollerblade', 'wagon', 'cart', 'ambulance', 'firetruck',
        'policecar', 'spaceship', 'rocket', 'shuttle', 'glider', 'sled', 'snowmobile'
    }

    # Find the last prepositional phrase (look for 'in', 'on', 'at', 'to', 'from', 'with', 'about', 'for', 'of', 'by', 'under', 'over', 'between', 'among', 'through', 'during', 'before', 'after', 'above', 'below', 'near', 'inside', 'outside', 'around', 'beside', 'upon', 'within', 'without', 'across', 'behind', 'along', 'against', 'toward', 'onto', 'off', 'down', 'up', 'past')
    prepositions = [
        'in', 'on', 'at', 'to', 'from', 'with', 'about', 'for', 'of', 'by', 'under', 'over',
        'between', 'among', 'through', 'during', 'before', 'after', 'above', 'below', 'near',
        'inside', 'outside', 'around', 'beside', 'upon', 'within', 'without', 'across',
        'behind', 'along', 'against', 'toward', 'onto', 'off', 'down', 'up', 'past'
    ]
    words = text.strip().split()
    last_prep_idx = -1
    for i in range(len(words)-1, -1, -1):
        if words[i].lower() in prepositions:
            last_prep_idx = i
            break
    if last_prep_idx == -1 or last_prep_idx == len(words)-1:
        return False  # No prepositional phrase found or no noun after preposition

    # Get the noun(s) after the preposition, up to punctuation or end
    noun = None
    for j in range(last_prep_idx+1, len(words)):
        w = words[j].strip('.,;:!?').lower()
        if w:  # skip empty
            noun = w  # last noun candidate

    if noun is None:
        return False

    # Remove plural 's' for matching
    if noun.endswith('s') and noun[:-1] in abstract_nouns | buildings | vehicles:
        noun = noun[:-1]

    if noun in abstract_nouns or noun in buildings or noun in vehicles:

        'lies in', 'lies on', 'lies inside',
        'remained in', 'remained on', 'remained inside',
        'sits atop', 'rests atop', 'lies atop'
    ]
    # Find the main noun: look for the first occurrence of any of the nouns
    main_noun = None
    words = t.replace('.', ' ').replace(',', ' ').split()
    for w in words:
        if w in nouns:
            main_noun = w
            break
    if not main_noun:
        return False
    # Check if the noun is described as stationary/stored
    # Look for phrases like "rice is in", "bread was stored", etc.
    for phrase in stationary_phrases:
        if f"{main_noun} {phrase}" in t:
            return True
    # Also check for "the rice is in", "the bread was stored", etc.
    for phrase in stationary_phrases:
        if f"the {main_noun} {phrase}" in t:
            return True
    # Also check for "rice sits in", "bread rests on", etc.
    for verb in ['sits', 'rests', 'lies', 'remains']:
        for prep in ['in', 'on', 'inside', 'atop']:
            if f"{main_noun} {verb} {prep}" in t or f"the {main_noun} {verb} {prep}" in t:
                return True
    return False

        'ment', 'ness', 'tion', 'sion', 'ity', 'ance', 'ence', 'ship', 'er', 'or',

    )

    if last_word.endswith("'s"):
        last_word = last_word[:-2]
    elif last_word.endswith("s'"):
        last_word = last_word[:-2]
    # Remove plural 's'
    if last_word.endswith('s') and len(last_word) > 3:
        last_word_singular = last_word[:-1]
    else:
        last_word_singular = last_word
    # Heuristic: check if last word is a person noun or abstract noun
    if last_word in person_nouns or last_word_singular in person_nouns:
        return False
    if last_word in abstract_nouns or last_word_singular in abstract_nouns:
        return False
    # Heuristic: check if last word looks like a noun (ends with common noun suffix)

        # But check if it's not abstract
        if last_word in abstract_nouns or last_word_singular in abstract_nouns:

        return True
    # Heuristic: if the last word is a common concrete noun (not exhaustive)
    concrete_nouns = {

    # Otherwise, return False
    return False

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # List of common prepositions
    prepositions = [
        'about', 'above', 'across', 'after', 'against', 'along', 'among', 'around', 'at', 'before', 'behind', 'below',
        'beneath', 'beside', 'between', 'beyond', 'by', 'despite', 'down', 'during', 'except', 'for', 'from', 'in',
        'inside', 'into', 'near', 'of', 'off', 'on', 'onto', 'out', 'outside', 'over', 'past', 'since', 'through',
        'throughout', 'to', 'toward', 'under', 'underneath', 'until', 'up', 'upon', 'with', 'within', 'without'
    ]
    # List of common concrete, countable, and specific physical objects/places
    concrete_nouns = [
        'table', 'chair', 'car', 'dog', 'cat', 'house', 'building', 'tree', 'book', 'phone', 'computer', 'bag',
        'box', 'door', 'window', 'street', 'room', 'city', 'park', 'school', 'bridge', 'river', 'mountain', 'island',
        'cup', 'bottle', 'pen', 'desk', 'bed', 'shoe', 'road', 'bus', 'train', 'station', 'airport', 'hotel', 'store',
        'shop', 'restaurant', 'kitchen', 'bathroom', 'garden', 'garage', 'museum', 'library', 'hospital', 'beach',
        'lake', 'forest', 'farm', 'market', 'office', 'factory', 'tower', 'plaza', 'stadium', 'theater', 'cafe'
    ]
    # List of common abstract, uncountable, or non-specific nouns
    abstract_nouns = [
        'love', 'happiness', 'information', 'music', 'air', 'water', 'sand', 'advice', 'news', 'furniture', 'money',
        'time', 'energy', 'power', 'freedom', 'justice', 'health', 'education', 'knowledge', 'safety', 'beauty',
        'weather', 'traffic', 'equipment', 'luggage', 'baggage', 'bread', 'rice', 'sugar', 'milk', 'oil', 'salt'
    ]
    # Lowercase for matching
    text_l = text.lower()
    # Find the first preposition in the text
    words = text_l.split()
    prep_idx = -1
    prep = ''
    for i, w in enumerate(words):
        if w in prepositions:
            prep_idx = i
            prep = w
            break
    if prep_idx == -1 or prep_idx == len(words) - 1:
        return False  # No preposition or nothing after preposition
    # Get the noun phrase after the preposition (up to next preposition or end)
    after = words[prep_idx+1:]
    for j, w in enumerate(after):
        if w in prepositions:
            after = after[:j]
            break
    # Remove determiners and adjectives (simple approach)
    determiners = {'the', 'a', 'an', 'this', 'that', 'these', 'those', 'my', 'your', 'his', 'her', 'its', 'our', 'their', 'some', 'any'}
    noun_phrase = [w for w in after if w not in determiners]
    if not noun_phrase:
        return False
    # Take the last word as the head noun
    head_noun = noun_phrase[-1]
    # Check for plural (simple heuristic: ends with 's' and not 'ss')
    is_plural = head_noun.endswith('s') and not head_noun.endswith('ss')
    # Check if head noun is in concrete nouns (singular or plural)
    if is_plural and head_noun[:-1] in concrete_nouns:
        return True
    if head_noun in concrete_nouns:
        return True
    # If head noun is in abstract or uncountable nouns, return False
    if head_noun in abstract_nouns:
        return False
    # If head noun is a mass noun (uncountable), return False
    mass_noun_endings = ['ness', 'tion', 'ment', 'ity', 'ship', 'hood', 'age', 'ance', 'ence', 'ism', 'ure', 'ing']
    if any(head_noun.endswith(suf) for suf in mass_noun_endings):
        return False
    # If head noun is a place (ends with 'city', 'town', 'village', 'park', etc.)
    place_endings = ['city', 'town', 'village', 'park', 'room', 'hall', 'station', 'school', 'office', 'hotel', 'store', 'shop', 'restaurant', 'kitchen', 'bathroom', 'garden', 'garage', 'museum', 'library', 'hospital', 'beach', 'lake', 'forest', 'farm', 'market', 'factory', 'tower', 'plaza', 'stadium', 'theater', 'cafe']
    if any(head_noun.endswith(suf) for suf in place_endings):
        return True
    # If noun phrase is a specific object (has a determiner or proper noun)
    if words[prep_idx+1] in determiners:
        return True
    # If head noun is capitalized in original text, assume it's a specific place/object
    orig_words = text.split()
    if prep_idx+1 < len(orig_words) and orig_words[prep_idx+1][0].isupper():
        return True
    return False

    objects_places = {

    }
    # List of common animate nouns, body parts, groups, and abstract concepts
    animate_or_abstract = {

        'nurse', 'police', 'officer', 'soldier', 'friend', 'enemy', 'family', 'team', 'group', 'crowd',
        'committee', 'audience', 'staff', 'body', 'head', 'hand', 'arm', 'leg', 'foot', 'eye', 'ear',

        'project', 'plan', 'goal', 'purpose', 'reason', 'result', 'effect', 'cause', 'change', 'growth'
    }
    # Remove punctuation from the end
    text = text.strip()
    while text and text[-1] in '.!?':
        text = text[:-1]

    words = text.split()
    if not words:
        return False
    # Find the last noun-like word (skip pronouns, prepositions, articles)
    skip = {'the', 'a', 'an', 'in', 'on', 'at', 'by', 'with', 'for', 'to', 'from', 'of', 'and', 'or', 'but', 'as', 'if', 'then', 'than', 'so', 'because', 'that', 'this', 'these', 'those', 'he', 'she', 'it', 'they', 'we', 'you', 'i', 'me', 'him', 'her', 'us', 'them', 'my', 'your', 'his', 'their', 'our', 'its', 'mine', 'yours', 'hers', 'ours', 'theirs'}
    # Go backwards to find the last noun candidate

        if not w or w in skip:

        # Heuristic: if ends with 'ing', 'ed', likely not a noun (but 'building' is a noun)
        if w.endswith('ing') and w not in objects_places:
            continue
        # If it's in animate/abstract, return False
        if w in animate_or_abstract:

        # If it's in objects/places, return True

        if any(w.endswith(suf) for suf in ['ness', 'tion', 'ment', 'ity', 'ship', 'ance', 'ence']):

        # Heuristic: if ends with 's', could be plural, check singular
        if w.endswith('s'):

        # Otherwise, default to False (unknown or likely not inanimate/concrete)

    # If no prepositional phrase found, return False

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # Helper to check if verb is passive (ends with -ed)
    def is_passive(verb):
        return verb.lower().endswith('ed')
    
    # Helper to extract subject, verb, and prepositional phrase
    def parse(text):
        words = text.strip().split()
        # Find first verb (assume it's the second word for simplicity)
        # Find first preposition (in, on, at, by, near, with, for, to, from, of, under, over, etc.)
        prepositions = {'in', 'on', 'at', 'by', 'near', 'with', 'for', 'to', 'from', 'of', 'under', 'over', 'after', 'before', 'about', 'against', 'between', 'into', 'through', 'during', 'without', 'within', 'along', 'across', 'behind', 'beyond', 'plus', 'except', 'but', 'up', 'out', 'around', 'down', 'off', 'above', 'below'}
        subj = ''
        verb = ''
        prep_phrase = ''
        # Very simple parsing: subject is first word, verb is second, rest is prepositional phrase
        if len(words) < 2:
            return '', '', ''
        subj = words[0]
        verb = words[1]
        # Find where prepositional phrase starts
        prep_start = -1
        for i, w in enumerate(words[2:], 2):
            if w.lower() in prepositions:
                prep_start = i
                break
        if prep_start != -1:
            prep_phrase = ' '.join(words[prep_start:])
        return subj, verb, prep_phrase

    # List of common subject-verb pairs (very limited for built-in only)
    common_pairs = {
        ('dog', 'barks'), ('cat', 'meows'), ('bird', 'flies'), ('child', 'plays'),
        ('teacher', 'teaches'), ('student', 'studies'), ('car', 'moves'), ('sun', 'rises'),
        ('rain', 'falls'), ('wind', 'blows'), ('fish', 'swims'), ('baby', 'cries'),
        ('man', 'walks'), ('woman', 'runs'), ('horse', 'gallops'), ('clock', 'ticks'),
        ('phone', 'rings'), ('computer', 'computes'), ('chef', 'cooks'), ('doctor', 'heals'),
        ('artist', 'paints'), ('driver', 'drives'), ('singer', 'sings'), ('writer', 'writes'),
        ('dog', 'runs'), ('cat', 'sleeps'), ('bird', 'sings'), ('child', 'learns'),
        ('teacher', 'explains'), ('student', 'reads'), ('car', 'stops'), ('sun', 'sets'),
        ('rain', 'stops'), ('wind', 'howls'), ('fish', 'jumps'), ('baby', 'laughs'),
        ('man', 'talks'), ('woman', 'smiles'), ('horse', 'eats'), ('clock', 'chimes'),
        ('phone', 'vibrates'), ('computer', 'crashes'), ('chef', 'bakes'), ('doctor', 'operates'),
        ('artist', 'draws'), ('driver', 'waits'), ('singer', 'performs'), ('writer', 'types'),
    }
    # Accept both singular and plural forms for subject and verb
    def normalize(word):
        return word.lower().rstrip('s')
    # Main logic
    subj, verb, prep_phrase = parse(text)
    if not subj or not verb:
        return False
    # Check if subject and verb are a common pair
    pair_common = (normalize(subj), normalize(verb)) in set((normalize(s), normalize(v)) for s, v in common_pairs)
    # Check for special prepositional phrases
    prep_phrase_l = prep_phrase.lower()
    special_phrases = ['by night', 'at dawn', 'near home']
    has_special = any(phrase in prep_phrase_l for phrase in special_phrases)
    passive = is_passive(verb)
    # Rule: True iff subject and verb do NOT typically go together,
    # and prepositional phrase does NOT contain special phrases unless verb is passive
    if not pair_common:
        if has_special and not passive:
            return False
        return True
    else:
        return False

    # For simplicity, assume subject is the first word, verb is the second
    subject = words[0].lower()
    verb = ''
    # Try to find the verb (first word after subject that is not an article or preposition)
    for w in words[1:]:
        lw = w.lower().strip('.,!?')
        if lw not in ('a', 'an', 'the', 'to', 'of', 'in', 'on', 'at', 'with', 'for', 'by', 'from', 'as', 'and', 'but', 'or', 'so', 'yet'):
            verb = lw
            break

    # Try to match subject to known roles
    matched_role = None
    for role in typical_roles:
        if subject == role or subject.endswith('s') and subject[:-1] == role:
            matched_role = role
            break

    if not matched_role or not verb:
        return True  # If we can't match, assume not typical

    # Check if verb matches typical verbs for the subject
    for v in typical_roles[matched_role]:
        if verb.startswith(v):  # Allow for simple tense/ending variations
            return False  # Typical role

    return True  # Not typical

    pleasant_words = ['pleasant', 'wonderful', 'delightful', 'enjoyable', 'lovely', 'nice', 'excellent', 'fantastic', 'amazing', 'great', 'superb', 'marvelous', 'splendid']
    awful_words = ['awful', 'poor', 'terrible', 'horrible', 'bad', 'dreadful', 'unpleasant', 'disgusting', 'lousy', 'atrocious', 'abysmal', 'appalling', 'wretched']
    pleasant = any(word in text_lower for word in pleasant_words)
    awful = any(word in text_lower for word in awful_words)
    if pleasant and not awful:
        return True
    if awful and not pleasant:
        return False
    # If both or neither, default to False
    return False

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # Define forbidden phrases
    forbidden_phrases = ["by hand", "on foot", "by day", "at dusk"]

    # Lowercase text for phrase search
    text_lower = text.lower()
    for phrase in forbidden_phrases:
        if phrase in text_lower:
            return False

    # Heuristic: Assume subject is the first word or phrase before a verb or preposition
    # List of common verbs and prepositions to split on
    verbs_preps = [
        "is", "was", "were", "are", "am", "be", "being", "been",
        "has", "have", "had", "will", "shall", "would", "should", "can", "could", "may", "might", "must", "do", "does", "did",
        "in", "on", "at", "by", "with", "from", "to", "for", "of", "about", "as", "into", "like", "through", "after", "over", "between", "out", "against", "during", "without", "before", "under", "around", "among"
    ]
    words = text.strip().split()
    subject_words = []
    for word in words:
        if word.lower() in verbs_preps:
            break
        subject_words.append(word)
    if not subject_words:
        return False
    subject = " ".join(subject_words)

    # Heuristic for proper noun:
    # - Person: First word capitalized, possibly two words (e.g., "John", "Mary Smith")
    # - City: Common city names or capitalized word
    # - Group: Capitalized multi-word phrase (e.g., "The Beatles", "United Nations")
    # We'll check if the subject is capitalized and not a common noun/pronoun

    # List of common pronouns and articles
    not_proper = {
        "i", "you", "he", "she", "it", "we", "they", "me", "him", "her", "us", "them",
        "my", "your", "his", "her", "its", "our", "their",
        "the", "a", "an", "this", "that", "these", "those"
    }

    # Remove punctuation from subject for checking
    subject_clean = "".join(c for c in subject if c.isalnum() or c.isspace())
    subject_words_clean = subject_clean.split()
    if not subject_words_clean:
        return False
    # If first word is not capitalized or is a pronoun/article, not a proper noun
    if subject_words_clean[0].lower() in not_proper:
        return False
    if not subject_words_clean[0][0].isupper():
        return False
    # If subject is more than one word, check if all words are capitalized (for groups/cities)
    if len(subject_words_clean) > 1:
        if not all(w[0].isupper() for w in subject_words_clean):
            return False

    return True

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # Forbidden phrases
    forbidden = ["by hand", "on foot", "by day", "at dusk"]

    # Check for forbidden phrases (case-insensitive)
    lowered = text.lower()
    for phrase in forbidden:
        if phrase in lowered:
            return False

    # Heuristic: Assume subject is the first word or phrase before a verb or preposition
    # We'll consider the first word as the subject for simplicity
    words = text.strip().split()
    if not words:
        return False

    first_word = words[0]
    # Proper noun: starts with uppercase, not all uppercase (to avoid acronyms)
    if first_word[0].isupper() and not first_word.isupper():
        return True

    # Also handle multi-word proper nouns (e.g., "New York", "The Beatles")
    # If the first two words are both capitalized, likely a proper noun group/place
    if len(words) > 1 and words[0][0].isupper() and words[1][0].isupper():
        return True

    return False

    # Check for plural (simple heuristic: ends with 's' and not possessive/apostrophe)

        return word.endswith('s') and not word.endswith("'s") and len(word) > 2

    # Check for collective noun
    if t in collective_nouns:
        return True
    # Check for profession

    if t in months:

    if is_plural(t):
        return True
    return False

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # Helper lists (not exhaustive, but for demonstration)
    occupations = {
        'doctor', 'engineer', 'teacher', 'nurse', 'lawyer', 'artist', 'scientist',
        'manager', 'chef', 'pilot', 'driver', 'farmer', 'police', 'firefighter',
        'student', 'professor', 'accountant', 'mechanic', 'plumber', 'carpenter',
        'dentist', 'architect', 'waiter', 'waitress', 'actor', 'actress', 'musician',
        'soldier', 'judge', 'clerk', 'cashier', 'receptionist', 'librarian', 'baker'
    }
    abstract_concepts = {
        'freedom', 'justice', 'love', 'happiness', 'truth', 'beauty', 'wisdom',
        'courage', 'honesty', 'peace', 'anger', 'sadness', 'knowledge', 'power',
        'friendship', 'loyalty', 'faith', 'hope', 'charity', 'patience', 'success',
        'failure', 'trust', 'fear', 'pride', 'envy', 'greed', 'joy', 'respect',
        'integrity', 'compassion', 'ambition', 'despair', 'destiny', 'luck'
    }

    t = text.strip()
    # Check for "Always"
    if t == "Always":
        return True

    # Lowercase for comparison
    t_lower = t.lower()

    # Remove leading articles
    for article in ('the ', 'a ', 'an '):
        if t_lower.startswith(article):
            t_lower = t_lower[len(article):]
            break

    # Remove trailing punctuation
    t_lower = t_lower.rstrip('.,!?')

    # Check if occupation or abstract concept (singular or plural)
    if t_lower in occupations or t_lower.rstrip('s') in occupations:
        return False
    if t_lower in abstract_concepts or t_lower.rstrip('s') in abstract_concepts:
        return False

    # Check for plural noun (simple heuristic: ends with 's' and not 'ss')
    # Exclude words that are likely not nouns (e.g., verbs, adjectives)
    # This is a naive check, but sufficient for the rule as stated
    if len(t_lower) > 2 and t_lower.endswith('s') and not t_lower.endswith('ss'):
        # Exclude if it's an occupation or abstract concept plural
        if t_lower in occupations or t_lower in abstract_concepts:
            return False
        return True

    return False

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # Define a small set of inanimate nouns and plausible action verbs
    inanimate_nouns = {
        'book', 'table', 'car', 'computer', 'rock', 'chair', 'door', 'window', 'phone', 'box',
        'paper', 'bottle', 'cup', 'bag', 'shoe', 'lamp', 'pen', 'pencil', 'key', 'coin'
    }
    plausible_actions = {
        ('book', 'table'): {'cover', 'hit', 'touch', 'support', 'hold', 'rest'},
        ('car', 'door'): {'hit', 'open', 'close', 'touch'},
        ('computer', 'table'): {'sit', 'rest', 'stand', 'be'},
        ('rock', 'window'): {'break', 'hit', 'crack', 'smash'},
        ('bottle', 'table'): {'stand', 'sit', 'rest', 'be'},
        ('box', 'table'): {'sit', 'rest', 'be'},
        ('pen', 'paper'): {'touch', 'mark', 'write', 'draw'},
        ('key', 'door'): {'open', 'unlock', 'touch', 'fit'},
        ('coin', 'table'): {'sit', 'rest', 'be', 'roll'},
        # Add more as needed
    }
    # For generalization, allow any inanimate subject/object with generic verbs
    generic_verbs = {'touch', 'hit', 'cover', 'support', 'hold', 'rest', 'sit', 'stand', 'be', 'open', 'close', 'break', 'crack', 'smash', 'mark', 'write', 'draw', 'unlock', 'fit', 'roll'}

    # Basic tokenization and parsing
    words = text.strip('.!?').split()
    if len(words) < 3:
        return False

    # Find subject (first noun), verb (first verb), object (first noun after verb)
    subject = None
    verb = None
    obj = None

    # Very naive POS tagging: assume first word is subject, second is verb, third is object
    # Try to handle "The book covers the table" or "A rock broke the window"
    # Remove determiners
    determiners = {'the', 'a', 'an', 'this', 'that', 'these', 'those', 'my', 'your', 'his', 'her', 'its', 'our', 'their'}
    idx = 0
    while idx < len(words) and words[idx].lower() in determiners:
        idx += 1
    if idx >= len(words):
        return False
    subject = words[idx].lower()
    idx += 1
    if idx >= len(words):
        return False
    verb = words[idx].lower()
    idx += 1
    # Skip determiners before object
    while idx < len(words) and words[idx].lower() in determiners:
        idx += 1
    if idx >= len(words):
        return False
    obj = words[idx].lower()

    # Check if subject and object are inanimate
    if subject not in inanimate_nouns or obj not in inanimate_nouns:
        return False

    # Check if verb is plausible for subject-object pair
    pair = (subject, obj)
    if pair in plausible_actions:
        if verb in plausible_actions[pair]:
            return True
    # Otherwise, check if verb is a generic plausible verb
    if verb in generic_verbs:
        return True

    return False

        if len(w) > 2 and (w.endswith('s') or w.endswith('ed') or w.endswith('ing')):

code:
def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # List of common inanimate objects and places (not exhaustive)
    inanimate_subjects = {
        "table", "tables", "car", "cars", "tree", "trees", "rock", "rocks", "book", "books",
        "city", "cities", "village", "villages", "building", "buildings", "house", "houses",
        "river", "rivers", "mountain", "mountains", "road", "roads", "school", "schools",
        "room", "rooms", "door", "doors", "window", "windows", "computer", "computers",
        "phone", "phones", "lamp", "lamps", "desk", "desks", "street", "streets", "park", "parks",
        "country", "countries", "island", "islands", "bridge", "bridges", "station", "stations",
        "airport", "airports", "library", "libraries", "museum", "museums", "garden", "gardens",
        "wall", "walls", "floor", "floors", "ceiling", "ceilings", "roof", "roofs", "lake", "lakes"
    }
    # List of human action verbs (base and common conjugations)
    human_verbs = {
        "walk", "walks", "walked", "walking",
        "talk", "talks", "talked", "talking",
        "live", "lives", "lived", "living",
        "play", "plays", "played", "playing",
        "smile", "smiles", "smiled", "smiling",
        "stay", "stays", "stayed", "staying",
        "help", "helps", "helped", "helping",
        "study", "studies", "studied", "studying",
        "prepare", "prepares", "prepared", "preparing",
        "arrive", "arrives", "arrived", "arriving",
        "decide", "decides", "decided", "deciding",
        "imagine", "imagines", "imagined", "imagining",
        "enjoy", "enjoys", "enjoyed", "enjoying"
    }
    # Basic sentence splitting (assume simple sentence, subject-verb-object)
    words = text.strip().split()
    if not words or len(words) < 2:
        return False
    # Remove leading articles from subject
    subject = words[0].lower()
    if subject in {"the", "a", "an"} and len(words) > 2:
        subject = words[1].lower()
        verb_index = 2
    else:
        verb_index = 1
    # Get verb (skip auxiliary verbs)
    verb = words[verb_index].lower() if len(words) > verb_index else ""
    # Check subject is inanimate and verb is not a human action
    if subject in inanimate_subjects and verb not in human_verbs:
        return True
    return False

    # Helper: get first word (subject), verb, and object (very basic)

    # Try to find subject, verb, object
    # Assume simple SVO: "The door opened", "My hand grabbed the cup"
    # Remove leading articles/possessives
    articles = {'the', 'a', 'an', 'my', 'your', 'his', 'her', 'its', 'our', 'their'}
    idx = 0
    while idx < len(words) and words[idx].lower() in articles:
        idx += 1
    if idx >= len(words):
        return False
    subject = words[idx].lower()
    # If subject is two words (e.g. 'front door', 'left hand')
    if idx+1 < len(words) and words[idx+1].lower() not in articles and words[idx+1][0].islower():
        subject += ' ' + words[idx+1].lower()
        idx += 1
    # Now verb is next word
    if idx+1 >= len(words):
        return False
    verb = words[idx+1].lower()
    # Object is next word after verb (skip articles)
    obj_idx = idx+2
    while obj_idx < len(words) and words[obj_idx].lower() in articles:
        obj_idx += 1
    if obj_idx < len(words):
        obj = words[obj_idx].lower()
    else:
        obj = ''
    # Check if subject is inanimate or body part
    # Try both full subject and last word (e.g. 'front door' -> 'door')
    subj_words = subject.split()
    subj_key = subject if subject in inanimate_objects else subj_words[-1]
    if subj_key not in inanimate_objects:
        return False
    # Check if verb is plausible for subject
    # Try base form of verb (remove 's', 'ed', 'ing')
    verb_base = verb
    if verb.endswith('ing'):
        verb_base = verb[:-3]
    elif verb.endswith('ed'):
        verb_base = verb[:-2]
    elif verb.endswith('es'):
        verb_base = verb[:-2]
    elif verb.endswith('s'):
        verb_base = verb[:-1]
    # Check plausible actions
    actions = plausible_actions.get(subj_key, set())
    if verb_base in actions:
        return True
    return False

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # List of verbs to check against
    forbidden_verbs = {"watched", "painted", "carried", "lifted", "closed", "sorted", "checked", "packed"}
    # Basic list of common adjectives (not exhaustive)
    adjectives = {
        "able", "bad", "best", "better", "big", "black", "blue", "certain", "clear", "different", "early", "easy",
        "economic", "federal", "free", "full", "good", "great", "hard", "high", "human", "important", "international",
        "large", "late", "little", "local", "long", "low", "major", "military", "national", "new", "old", "only",
        "other", "political", "possible", "public", "real", "recent", "right", "small", "social", "special", "strong",
        "sure", "true", "white", "whole", "young", "red", "green", "yellow", "brown", "quick", "slow", "happy",
        "sad", "angry", "tall", "short", "fat", "thin", "rich", "poor", "clean", "dirty", "soft", "hard", "hot",
        "cold", "warm", "cool", "sweet", "bitter", "salty", "sour", "loud", "quiet", "bright", "dark", "sharp",
        "dull", "heavy", "light", "fast", "slow", "strong", "weak", "young", "old", "new", "ancient", "modern",
        "beautiful", "ugly", "handsome", "pretty", "cute", "funny", "serious", "brave", "cowardly", "friendly",
        "mean", "kind", "cruel", "polite", "rude", "smart", "stupid", "clever", "silly", "honest", "dishonest"
    }
    # Remove punctuation for easier processing
    text_clean = ""
    for c in text:
        if c.isalnum() or c.isspace():
            text_clean += c
        else:
            text_clean += " "
    words = text_clean.split()
    # Find the subject (before the first verb)
    # For simplicity, assume the first verb is the main verb
    # We'll look for the first word ending with "ed" or "s" or "ing" or a forbidden verb
    verb_idx = -1
    for i, w in enumerate(words):
        lw = w.lower()
        if lw in forbidden_verbs or lw.endswith("ed") or lw.endswith("ing") or lw.endswith("s"):
            verb_idx = i
            break
    if verb_idx == -1:
        # No verb found, can't apply rule
        return False
    subject_words = words[:verb_idx]
    # Count adjectives in subject
    adj_count = 0
    for w in subject_words:
        if w.lower() in adjectives:
            adj_count += 1
    # Check verb
    verb = words[verb_idx].lower()
    if adj_count >= 2 and verb not in forbidden_verbs:
        return True
    return False

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    vowels = 'aeiou'
    def is_adjective(word):
        # Heuristic: adjectives often end with 'y', 'ive', 'ous', 'ful', 'less', 'al', 'ic', 'able', 'ible', 'ish', 'ent', 'ant'
        suffixes = ['y', 'ive', 'ous', 'ful', 'less', 'al', 'ic', 'able', 'ible', 'ish', 'ent', 'ant']
        return any(word.endswith(suf) for suf in suffixes)
    
    # Simple subject-verb-object split: assume first verb is the main verb
    words = text.strip().split()
    verbs = ['is', 'are', 'was', 'were', 'be', 'am', 'been', 'being', 'has', 'have', 'had', 'do', 'does', 'did',
             'will', 'would', 'shall', 'should', 'may', 'might', 'must', 'can', 'could', 'go', 'goes', 'went', 'gone',
             'see', 'sees', 'saw', 'seen', 'make', 'makes', 'made', 'get', 'gets', 'got', 'gotten', 'give', 'gives', 'gave', 'given',
             'take', 'takes', 'took', 'taken', 'eat', 'eats', 'ate', 'eaten', 'like', 'likes', 'liked', 'love', 'loves', 'loved',
             'hate', 'hates', 'hated', 'find', 'finds', 'found', 'say', 'says', 'said', 'tell', 'tells', 'told', 'think', 'thinks', 'thought']
    verb_idx = -1
    for i, w in enumerate(words):
        if w.lower() in verbs:
            verb_idx = i
            break
    if verb_idx == -1:
        # No verb found, treat all as subject, no object
        subject = words
        obj = []
    else:
        subject = words[:verb_idx]
        obj = words[verb_idx+1:]
    # Count adjectives in subject and object
    subj_adj = sum(is_adjective(w.lower().strip('.,;:!?')) for w in subject)
    obj_adj = sum(is_adjective(w.lower().strip('.,;:!?')) for w in obj)
    return subj_adj >= 2 or obj_adj >= 2

        "north", "south", "east", "west", "new", "old", "lake", "fort",
        "san", "santa", "los", "las", "el", "la", "saint", "st",
    }

    place_suffixes = (
        "ville", "burg", "burgh", "ton", "shire", "ford", "field",
        "land", "stan", "ia", "berg", "stadt", "grad", "pur", "abad",
        "polis", "ham", "wick", "mouth", "port", "dale", "bury",
    )

    lower_words = [w.lower().strip(".,") for w in words]

    # Check for explicit place keywords
    for w in lower_words:
        if w in place_keywords:
            return False

    # Check place suffixes on each word
    for w in lower_words:
        if len(w) > 4 and w.endswith(place_suffixes):
            return False

    # Person heuristic: title prefixes
    person_titles = {
        "mr", "mrs", "ms", "miss", "dr", "prof", "professor", "sir",
        "lady", "lord", "dame", "rev", "reverend", "president", "king",
        "queen", "prince", "princess", "captain", "major", "general",
        "colonel", "sergeant", "judge", "senator", "governor", "mayor",
    }
    if lower_words and lower_words[0].strip(".") in person_titles:
        return True

    # A name is typically 1-4 capitalized words, alphabetic
    if 1 <= len(words) <= 4:
        all_namelike = True
        for w in words:
            core = w.strip(".,'-")
            if not core:
                all_namelike = False
                break
            # Allow capitalized alphabetic words, or single initials
            if not (core[0].isupper() and all(c.isalpha() or c in "'-" for c in core)):
                all_namelike = False
                break
        if all_namelike:
            return True

    return False

code:
def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    words = text.split()
    
    # Clean words of punctuation for analysis
    def clean(w):
        return w.strip(".,!?;:\"'()[]{}").lower()
    
    cleaned = [clean(w) for w in words]
    
    # Check for verb ending in "ed"
    has_ed_verb = any(w.endswith("ed") and len(w) > 2 for w in cleaned)
    
    # Check for a noun (heuristic: any word that isn't clearly something else)
    # Simple approach: treat any alphabetic word not ending in "ly" or "ed" as a potential noun
    has_noun = any(w.isalpha() and not w.endswith("ly") for w in cleaned)
    
    # Count adverbs ending in "ly"
    ly_count = sum(1 for w in cleaned if w.endswith("ly") and len(w) > 2)
    
    return has_ed_verb and has_noun and ly_count <= 1

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    words = [w.strip(".,!?;:\"'()[]").lower() for w in text.split()]
    words = [w for w in words if w]

    # Find a verb ending in "ed" (heuristic: word ends with "ed", length > 3,
    # and isn't a known non-verb word)
    non_verbs_ed = {"red", "bed", "bled", "bread", "thread", "speed", "need",
                    "feed", "seed", "weed", "deed", "creed", "greed", "indeed",
                    "embed", "shed", "sled", "fled", "wed", "naked", "wicked",
                    "sacred", "hundred"}
    has_ed_verb = any(
        w.endswith("ed") and len(w) > 3 and w not in non_verbs_ed
        for w in words

    # Places and buildings to exclude
    places_buildings = {

        "store", "shop", "mall", "hospital", "library", "museum", "hotel",

        "letter", "phone", "computer", "machine", "tool", "toy", "gift",
        "key", "box", "bag", "hat", "shoe", "shirt", "coat", "dress",

    has_noun = any(w in common_nouns for w in words if w not in places_buildings)

    return has_ed_verb and has_noun

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    frequency = {"usually", "often", "always", "rarely", "sometimes",
                 "never", "seldom", "frequently", "occasionally", "normally",
                 "generally", "regularly"}
    location = {"downtown", "yesterday", "overhead", "nearby", "everywhere",
                "below", "upstairs", "inside", "outside", "downstairs",
                "above", "here", "there", "abroad", "indoors", "outdoors",
                "somewhere", "anywhere", "nowhere"}
    words = [w.strip(".,!?;:\"'()[]{}").lower() for w in text.split()]
    has_freq = any(w in frequency for w in words)
    has_loc = any(w in location for w in words)
    return has_freq and has_loc

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    keywords = {"downtown", "yesterday", "nearby", "everywhere", "below",
                "always", "rarely", "probably", "certainly", "clearly",
                "finally", "carefully", "quietly", "slowly", "often",
                "usually", "overhead", "recently", "luckily"}
    places = {"house", "lake", "shop", "store", "park", "school", "office",
              "city", "town", "village", "market", "river", "mountain",

              "hall", "hallway", "basement", "attic", "garage", "place",
              "downtown", "country", "nation", "state", "county"}

    words = []
    cur = ""
    for ch in text:
        if ch.isalpha():
            cur += ch
        else:
            if cur:
                words.append(cur)
                cur = ""
    if cur:
        words.append(cur)

    lower_words = [w.lower() for w in words]

    if not any(w in keywords for w in lower_words):
        return False

    nouns = {
        # common nouns including places
        "house", "lake", "shop", "store", "park", "school", "office",
        "city", "town", "village", "market", "river", "mountain",
        "beach", "garden", "field", "road", "street", "building",
        "hotel", "restaurant", "church", "library", "museum",

        "castle", "farm", "yard", "room", "kitchen", "bedroom",
        "hall", "hallway", "basement", "attic", "garage", "place",
        "country", "nation", "state", "county",
        "dog", "cat", "bird", "man", "woman", "child", "boy", "girl",
        "car", "tree", "book", "table", "chair", "ball", "phone",
        "computer", "door", "window", "cup", "plate", "box", "bag",
        "person", "people", "friend", "family", "teacher", "student",
        "doctor", "nurse", "worker", "player", "fish", "horse", "cow",
        "flower", "plant", "food", "water", "money", "time", "day",
        "night", "morning", "evening", "song", "music", "game", "story",
        "letter", "word", "number", "color", "animal", "thing",
    }

    last_noun = None
    for w in lower_words:
        if w in nouns:
            last_noun = w

    if last_noun is None:
        return True

    return last_noun not in places

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    words = text.strip().split()
    if not words:
        return False

    # Get the subject (first noun-like word, simplified to first word)
    subject = words[0].strip('.,!?;:"\'').lower()

    # Check plural: ends in 's' but not 'ss', or known irregular plurals
    irregular_plurals = {'people', 'children', 'men', 'women', 'mice', 'geese',
                         'feet', 'teeth', 'oxen'}
    is_plural = False
    if subject in irregular_plurals:
        is_plural = True
    elif subject.endswith('s') and not subject.endswith('ss') and len(subject) > 2:
        is_plural = True

    if not is_plural:
        return False

    # Exclude places
    places = {'cities', 'towns', 'countries', 'states', 'villages', 'islands',
              'mountains', 'rivers', 'lakes', 'oceans', 'beaches', 'forests',
              'parks', 'streets', 'roads', 'buildings', 'rooms', 'houses',

                   'chefs', 'waiters', 'singers', 'actors', 'dancers',
                   'painters', 'builders', 'players', 'students'}

    if subject in places or subject in professions:
        return False

    # Check for location prepositional phrase
    location_preps = {'in', 'on', 'at', 'near', 'under', 'over', 'above',
                      'below', 'beside', 'behind', 'beneath', 'inside',
                      'outside', 'between', 'among', 'across', 'around',
                      'by', 'within'}

    lowered = [w.strip('.,!?;:"\'').lower() for w in words]
    has_location_pp = False
    for i, w in enumerate(lowered):
        if w in location_preps and i + 1 < len(lowered):
            has_location_pp = True
            break

    return has_location_pp

The label is True if and only if the sentence contains exactly one prepositional phrase that is not followed by another prepositional phrase; otherwise, the label is False.
code:
def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    prepositions = {
        "aboard", "about", "above", "across", "after", "against", "along",
        "amid", "among", "around", "at", "before", "behind", "below",
        "beneath", "beside", "between", "beyond", "by", "down", "during",
        "except", "for", "from", "in", "inside", "into", "near", "of",
        "off", "on", "onto", "out", "outside", "over", "past", "since",
        "through", "throughout", "to", "toward", "towards", "under",
        "underneath", "until", "up", "upon", "with", "within", "without"
    }

    # tokenize into words, stripping punctuation
    words = []
    for raw in text.split():
        w = raw.strip(".,;:!?\"'()[]{}").lower()
        if w:
            words.append(w)

    # mark which positions start a preposition
    is_prep = [w in prepositions for w in words]

    # count prepositional phrases that are NOT immediately followed by
    # another prepositional phrase (i.e., a preposition not directly
    # followed by another preposition)
    count = 0
    for i, prep in enumerate(is_prep):
        if prep:
            # is the next word also a preposition?
            if i + 1 < len(is_prep) and is_prep[i + 1]:
                continue
            count += 1

    return count == 1

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # Heuristic: parse a "subject verb object" style sentence.

        "ant", "bee", "fly", "spider", "rabbit", "fox", "wolf", "deer",
    }

    containers = {
        "box", "drawer", "cabinet", "closet", "bag", "basket", "jar",
        "bottle", "cup", "bowl", "pot", "pan", "fridge", "refrigerator",
        "oven", "shelf", "table", "desk", "room", "kitchen", "garage",
        "house", "building", "cupboard", "chest", "trunk", "case",
        "container", "bin", "bucket", "crate", "wallet", "purse",
        "pocket", "envelope", "folder", "cabinet", "vase", "tank",
        "barrel", "safe", "locker", "suitcase", "backpack", "pantry",
        "shed", "warehouse", "attic", "basement", "cellar", "freezer",
        "sink", "tray", "plate", "glass", "mug", "carton", "package",
    }

    small_items = {
        "key", "pen", "pencil", "book", "phone", "coin", "ring", "cup",
        "spoon", "fork", "knife", "ball", "toy", "apple", "orange",
        "letter", "card", "watch", "clock", "lamp", "bottle", "cup",
        "plate", "bowl", "glass", "candle", "rock", "stone", "flower",
        "leaf", "paper", "wallet", "money", "bill", "note", "ticket",
        "tool", "hammer", "nail", "screw", "wire", "battery", "lamp",
        "shoe", "sock", "hat", "shirt", "towel", "blanket", "pillow",
        "cookie", "bread", "cheese", "milk", "water", "juice", "food",
        "jewel", "gem", "diamond", "necklace", "bracelet", "glasses",
    }

    words = [w.strip(".,!?;:'\"()").lower() for w in text.split()]
    words = [w for w in words if w]
    if len(words) < 2:
        return False

    subject = words[0]
    obj = words[-1]

    # both must be inanimate
    if subject in animate or obj in animate:
        return False

    # subject should be a movable/storable item
    if subject not in small_items and subject not in containers:
        return False

    # object should be a plausible container/location
    if obj not in containers:
        return False

    # a container can't plausibly hold itself
    if subject == obj:
        return False

    return True

        if w in rel_words:
            rel_idx = i
            break

        if w in inanimate:
            subject = w

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    words = text.replace(",", " ").replace(".", " ").replace("!", " ").replace("?", " ").split()
    if len(words) < 2:
        return False

    lower = [w.lower() for w in words]

    # Strip leading article/determiner to find subject
    articles = {"the", "a", "an", "this", "that", "these", "those", "my", "your", "his", "her", "its", "our", "their"}

    idx = 0
    if lower[0] in articles:
        idx = 1
    if idx >= len(lower):
        return False

    subject = lower[idx]

    # Concrete, inanimate, countable nouns
    concrete_inanimate = {
        "rock", "stone", "ball", "car", "book", "pen", "cup", "plate", "table",
        "chair", "door", "window", "clock", "bell", "drum", "phone", "computer",
        "lamp", "key", "box", "bottle", "wheel", "engine", "machine", "robot",
        "train", "bus", "boat", "ship", "plane", "rocket", "fan", "radio",
        "speaker", "alarm", "bottle", "coin", "stick", "knife", "fork", "spoon",
        "hammer", "saw", "wrench", "bolt", "screw", "gear", "spring", "pendulum",
        "magnet", "battery", "wire", "cable", "bulb", "candle", "match", "torch",
        "umbrella", "shovel", "broom", "bucket", "barrel", "crate", "kettle",
        "pot", "pan", "oven", "toaster", "blender", "mixer", "vacuum", "printer",
        "camera", "tv", "television", "screen", "monitor", "keyboard", "mouse",
        "door", "gate", "valve", "pipe", "tank", "pump", "motor", "propeller",
        "blade", "fan", "siren", "horn", "whistle", "trumpet", "piano", "guitar",

        "rope", "chain", "anchor", "sail", "flag", "banner", "kite", "balloon",
        "wagon", "cart", "tractor", "crane", "drill", "fountain", "geyser"
    }

    # Living / abstract / uncountable / mass nouns -> not allowed
    not_concrete_inanimate = {
        "dog", "cat", "man", "woman", "boy", "girl", "person", "people", "bird",
        "fish", "child", "baby", "teacher", "student", "doctor", "horse", "cow",
        "love", "idea", "freedom", "hope", "anger", "joy", "water", "air", "sand",
        "rice", "milk", "music", "time", "money", "information", "advice", "snow",
        "rain", "wind", "fire", "light", "sun", "moon", "sky", "ocean", "sea"
    }

    if subject in not_concrete_inanimate:
        return False
    if subject not in concrete_inanimate:
        return False

    # Find a verb after the subject
    verb_idx = idx + 1
    if verb_idx >= len(lower):
        return False
    verb = lower[verb_idx]

    # Verbs that require animacy / agency (sentient action) -> implausible
    animate_verbs = {
        "think", "thinks", "thought", "believe", "believes", "feel", "feels",
        "love", "loves", "hate", "hates", "want", "wants", "know", "knows",
        "eat", "eats", "ate", "drink", "drinks", "sleep", "sleeps", "speak",

        "dream", "dreams", "hope", "hopes", "wish", "wishes", "remember", "remembers"
    }

    if verb in animate_verbs:
        return False

    # Plausible inanimate verbs (motion, sound, physical actions)
    plausible_verbs = {
        "fell", "falls", "fall", "rolled", "rolls", "roll", "broke", "breaks",

        "ticked", "ticks", "tick", "chimed", "chimes", "chime", "lit", "lights",

        "slid", "slides", "slide", "tipped", "tips", "tip", "swung", "swings",

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # Sets of words to classify the object of the preposition
    prepositions = {
        "about", "above", "across", "after", "against", "along", "among",
        "around", "at", "before", "behind", "below", "beneath", "beside",
        "between", "beyond", "by", "down", "during", "for", "from", "in",
        "inside", "into", "near", "of", "off", "on", "onto", "out", "outside",
        "over", "past", "since", "through", "throughout", "to", "toward",
        "towards", "under", "underneath", "until", "up", "upon", "with",
        "within", "without"
    }

    # People, animals, body parts -> these make the rule False
    animate_or_body = {
        # people
        "person", "people", "man", "men", "woman", "women", "child",
        "children", "kid", "kids", "boy", "boys", "girl", "girls", "baby",
        "father", "mother", "dad", "mom", "parent", "parents", "son",

        "family", "crowd", "team", "boss", "leader", "judge", "lawyer",
        "everyone", "someone", "anyone", "nobody", "somebody", "anybody",
        "him", "her", "them", "me", "us", "you", "he", "she", "they",
        "uncle", "aunt", "cousin", "grandfather", "grandmother", "husband",
        "wife", "actor", "actress", "singer", "author", "artist", "chef",
        # animals
        "dog", "dogs", "cat", "cats", "bird", "birds", "fish", "horse",
        "horses", "cow", "cows", "pig", "pigs", "sheep", "lion", "tiger",
        "bear", "bears", "wolf", "wolves", "fox", "deer", "rabbit", "mouse",
        "rat", "snake", "frog", "duck", "chicken", "hen", "rooster", "goat",
        "elephant", "monkey", "ape", "whale", "dolphin", "shark", "insect",
        "bee", "ant", "spider", "butterfly", "animal", "animals", "puppy",
        "kitten", "calf", "lamb", "owl", "eagle", "hawk", "crow", "creature",
        # body parts
        "head", "hand", "hands", "arm", "arms", "leg", "legs", "foot",
        "feet", "eye", "eyes", "ear", "ears", "nose", "mouth", "face",
        "finger", "fingers", "toe", "toes", "hair", "neck", "shoulder",
        "shoulders", "chest", "back", "knee", "elbow", "lip", "lips",
        "tooth", "teeth", "tongue", "heart", "brain", "stomach", "skin",
        "bone", "bones", "blood", "throat", "cheek", "chin", "wrist",
        "ankle", "thumb", "palm", "body"
    }

    words = []
    for w in text.lower().split():
        cleaned = "".join(c for c in w if c.isalpha())
        if cleaned:
            words.append(cleaned)

    # Find a prepositional phrase: preposition followed by a noun
    found_object = None
    articles = {"the", "a", "an", "this", "that", "these", "those",
                "my", "your", "his", "her", "its", "our", "their", "some",
                "any", "no", "each", "every"}

    for i, w in enumerate(words):
        if w in prepositions:
            # look at the following words to find the head noun
            j = i + 1
            while j < len(words) and words[j] in articles:
                j += 1
            if j < len(words):
                found_object = words[j]
                break

    if found_object is None:
        return False

    # If the object is animate or a body part, return False
    if found_object in animate_or_body:
        return False

    return True

    words = text.lower().replace(",", " ").replace(".", " ").replace("!", " ").replace("?", " ").split()

    last_prep_idx = -1
    for i, w in enumerate(words):
        if w in prepositions:
            last_prep_idx = i

    if last_prep_idx == -1 or last_prep_idx == len(words) - 1:
        return False

    skip = {"the", "a", "an", "this", "that", "these", "those", "my", "your",
            "his", "her", "its", "our", "their", "some", "any", "many", "few"}

    target = None
    for w in words[last_prep_idx + 1:]:
        clean = w.strip("'\"")
        if clean in skip:
            continue
        target = clean

    if target is None:
        return False

    candidates = {target}
    if target.endswith("s") and len(target) > 1:
        candidates.add(target[:-1])
    if target.endswith("es") and len(target) > 2:
        candidates.add(target[:-2])
    if target.endswith("ies") and len(target) > 3:
        candidates.add(target[:-3] + "y")

        if c in abstract or c in buildings or c in vehicles:
            return True
    return False

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    t = text.lower()

    nouns = ["rice", "bread", "soup"]
    found = None
    for n in nouns:
        # match as a whole word
        idx = t.find(n)
        while idx != -1:
            before = t[idx-1] if idx > 0 else " "
            after = t[idx+len(n)] if idx+len(n) < len(t) else " "
            if not before.isalpha() and not after.isalpha():
                found = n
                break
            idx = t.find(n, idx+1)
        if found:
            break

    if not found:
        return False

    # words suggesting stationary / stored in a location
    stationary_words = [
        "sits", "sit", "sitting", "sat",
        "stays", "stay", "staying", "stayed",
        "rests", "rest", "resting", "rested",
        "stored", "store", "storing",
        "stands", "stand", "standing", "stood",
        "lies", "lie", "lying", "lay",
        "remains", "remain", "remaining", "remained",
        "kept", "keeps", "keep", "keeping",
        "placed", "place", "placing",
        "left", "is on", "is in", "was on", "was in",
        "in the", "on the", "inside", "within",
        "is being stored", "is kept", "waiting",
    ]

    # movement words that indicate it is NOT stationary
    motion_words = [
        "moving", "moves", "moved", "carried", "carrying",
        "thrown", "throws", "throwing", "falling", "fell",
        "spilling", "spilled", "poured", "pouring",
        "flying", "running", "rolling", "sliding",
    ]

    for m in motion_words:
        if m in t:
            return False

    for w in stationary_words:
        if w in t:
            return True

    return False

    # Extract the last word (strip punctuation)
    s = text.strip()
    # remove trailing punctuation
    while s and not (s[-1].isalnum()):

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # Prepositions to look for
    preps = {
        "in", "on", "at", "to", "from", "by", "with", "into", "onto",
        "under", "over", "near", "beside", "behind", "above", "below",
        "across", "through", "toward", "towards", "inside", "outside",
        "beneath", "between", "among", "around", "upon", "off", "of"
    }

    # Abstract / mass nouns that are NOT concrete countable specific objects
    abstract = {
        "love", "time", "happiness", "freedom", "idea", "thought", "hope",
        "fear", "anger", "joy", "sadness", "knowledge", "information",
        "music", "water", "air", "money", "peace", "war", "truth", "beauty",
        "justice", "power", "energy", "light", "darkness", "life", "death",
        "work", "play", "fun", "luck", "faith", "trust", "honor", "pride",
        "courage", "wisdom", "weather", "rain", "snow", "wind", "fire",
        "history", "science", "art", "nature", "society", "culture",
        "future", "past", "present", "mind", "soul", "spirit", "heart",
        "advice", "news", "research", "evidence", "progress", "success",
        "failure", "experience", "freedom", "wealth", "health", "education",
        "morning", "evening", "night", "afternoon", "day", "week", "month",
        "year", "moment", "hour", "minute", "second", "everything",
        "something", "anything", "nothing", "it", "them", "us", "him", "her",
        "this", "that", "these", "those", "thing", "things"
    }

    # Words suggesting concrete physical objects/places
    concrete_hints = {
        "table", "chair", "house", "car", "book", "tree", "box", "door",
        "window", "room", "kitchen", "garden", "park", "store", "shop",
        "school", "office", "building", "bridge", "river", "lake", "mountain",
        "road", "street", "city", "town", "village", "beach", "forest",
        "desk", "bed", "sofa", "cup", "glass", "bottle", "plate", "bowl",
        "phone", "computer", "lamp", "wall", "floor", "ceiling", "roof",
        "garage", "barn", "shelf", "drawer", "cabinet", "fridge", "oven",
        "stove", "sink", "bathroom", "bedroom", "hall", "stairs", "gate",
        "fence", "yard", "field", "farm", "island", "hill", "valley",
        "church", "hospital", "library", "museum", "station", "airport",
        "restaurant", "cafe", "bar", "hotel", "market", "mall", "bank",
        "rock", "stone", "chair", "ball", "bag", "knife", "fork", "spoon",
        "pen", "pencil", "paper", "card", "key", "lock", "clock", "mirror",
        "picture", "painting", "statue", "tower", "castle", "wall", "boat",
        "ship", "plane", "train", "bus", "truck", "bike", "bicycle"
    }

    words = text.lower().replace(".", " ").replace(",", " ").split()

    # Find the last preposition and the noun phrase after it
    found_prep_index = -1
    for i, w in enumerate(words):
        if w in preps:
            found_prep_index = i

    if found_prep_index == -1 or found_prep_index == len(words) - 1:
        return False

    after = words[found_prep_index + 1:]

    # Skip articles/possessives/adjectives to find head noun candidates
    skip = {"the", "a", "an", "my", "your", "his", "her", "its", "our",
            "their", "this", "that", "these", "those", "some", "any"}

    # The head noun is typically the last noun-like word in the phrase
    candidates = [w for w in after if w not in skip]
    if not candidates:
        return False

    head = candidates[-1].rstrip("s") if candidates[-1] not in concrete_hints else candidates[-1]

    # Check any word in phrase against abstract set
    for w in candidates:
        base = w.rstrip("s")
        if w in abstract or base in abstract:
            return False

    # Check for concrete hints
    for w in candidates:
        base = w.rstrip("s")
        if w in concrete_hints or base in concrete_hints:
            return True

    return False

    }

    acceptable = natural_objects_locations | body_parts

    words = text.lower().replace(",", " ").replace(".", " ").replace("!", " ").replace("?", " ").split()

    articles = {"the", "a", "an", "his", "her", "their", "its", "my",
                "your", "our", "this", "that", "these", "those", "some",
                "any", "each", "every", "no"}
    adjectives_skip = articles

    for i, w in enumerate(words):
        if w in prepositions:
            # find the object noun: skip determiners/adjectives
            j = i + 1

        "moths", "beetle", "beetles", "wasp", "wasps", "mosquito", "mosquitoes",
        "animal", "animals",
    }

    def tokenize(s):
        word = ""
        toks = []
        for ch in s.lower():
            if ch.isalpha() or ch == "'":
                word += ch
            else:
                if word:
                    toks.append(word)
                    word = ""
        if word:
            toks.append(word)
        return toks

    tokens = tokenize(text)
    if not tokens:
        return False

    # Find the subject: first noun-like token, typically after a leading article
    determiners = {"the", "a", "an", "this", "that", "these", "those", "my",
                   "your", "his", "her", "its", "our", "their", "some", "many",
                   "few", "several", "two", "three", "one"}

    for tok in tokens:
        if tok in determiners:
            continue
        return tok in animals

    return False
