def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    t = text.lower()

    colors = ["orange", "yellow", "turquoise", "purple"]
    adjectives = ["gentle", "shallow"]

    # Find which descriptor is used
    used_color = next((c for c in colors if c in t), None)
    used_adj = next((a for a in adjectives if a in t), None)

    # Plausibility checks for color + noun combinations
    if used_color:
        # Objects/places that can plausibly have these colors
        plausible_nouns = [
            "sky", "sunset", "sunrise", "flower", "fruit", "shirt",
            "dress", "ball", "car", "wall", "paint", "water", "sea",
            "ocean", "lake", "gem", "stone", "scarf", "balloon", "kite",
            "umbrella", "juice", "fish", "bird", "leaf", "leaves",
            "pumpkin", "carrot", "banana", "lemon", "sun", "grape",
            "lavender", "amethyst", "blanket", "curtain", "sweater",
            "jacket", "hat", "tie", "rug", "cushion", "pillow", "towel",
            "cup", "mug", "plate", "bowl", "bag", "shoe", "shoes",
            "marker", "crayon", "pencil", "house", "door", "fence",
            "roof", "light", "lamp", "bottle", "box", "book", "cover",
            "tile", "tiles", "pool", "river", "stream", "petal", "petals",
            "necklace", "ring", "bracelet", "feather", "butterfly",
            "candle", "cloth", "fabric", "table", "chair", "sofa",
            "blossom", "tulip", "rose", "orchid", "iris",
        ]
        # Implausible: things that cannot reasonably be these colors
        implausible_nouns = [
            "milk", "snow", "coal", "blood", "charcoal", "soot",
            "cloud", "polar bear", "ash", "ink",
        ]

        if any(n in t for n in implausible_nouns):
            return False
        if any(n in t for n in plausible_nouns):
            return True
        # Default: unclear color noun, treat as implausible
        return False

    if used_adj:
        # "gentle" applies to forces, motions, people, weather
        gentle_nouns = [
            "breeze", "wind", "wave", "waves", "rain", "touch", "voice",
            "hand", "hands", "person", "man", "woman", "smile", "slope",
            "stream", "river", "current", "music", "song", "rocking",
            "push", "nudge", "giant", "soul", "nature", "creature",
            "animal", "dog", "horse", "tap", "knock", "motion",
        ]
        # "shallow" applies to water/depth-related nouns
        shallow_nouns = [
            "water", "pool", "river", "lake", "sea", "stream", "pond",
            "end", "dish", "bowl", "grave", "breath", "breathing",
            "puddle", "creek", "basin", "channel", "depth", "valley",
            "trench", "well", "ditch",
        ]

        if used_adj == "gentle":
            return any(n in t for n in gentle_nouns)
        if used_adj == "shallow":
            return any(n in t for n in shallow_nouns)

    return False

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    words = text.lower().split()
    if not words:
        return False

    irregular_pp = {
        "broken", "done", "gone", "seen", "taken", "given", "written",
        "spoken", "eaten", "driven", "ridden", "frozen", "chosen",
        "stolen", "forgotten", "hidden", "beaten", "fallen", "drawn",
        "thrown", "grown", "known", "flown", "blown", "shown", "worn",
        "torn", "sworn", "born", "begun", "drunk", "sung", "sunk",
        "swum", "run", "come", "become", "made", "built", "bought",
        "caught", "taught", "thought", "brought", "fought", "sought",
        "found", "bound", "held", "kept", "left", "met", "paid",
        "said", "sold", "told", "felt", "led", "read", "lost", "sent",
        "spent", "set", "let", "put", "cut", "hit", "shut", "hurt",
        "cost", "won", "shot", "got", "gotten",
    }

    occupation_action = {
        "doctor": "treated", "teacher": "taught", "chef": "cooked",
        "writer": "wrote", "driver": "drove", "singer": "sang",
        "builder": "built", "painter": "painted", "baker": "baked",
        "farmer": "farmed", "cleaner": "cleaned", "dancer": "danced",
        "player": "played", "runner": "ran", "swimmer": "swam",
        "pilot": "flew", "nurse": "treated", "artist": "drew",
    }

    def is_pp(w):
        return w.endswith("ed") or w in irregular_pp

    # find occupation in text
    occ = None
    for w in words:
        cw = w.strip(".,!?")
        if cw in occupation_action:
            occ = cw
            break

    # find a past participle verb
    pp = None
    for w in words:
        cw = w.strip(".,!?")
        if is_pp(cw):
            pp = cw
            break

    if pp is None:
        return False
    if occ is None:
        return True

    return pp != occupation_action[occ]

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    t = text.lower()
    words = t.replace(".", " ").replace(",", " ").split()

    # Typical subject-verb associations (subject commonly performs verb)
    typical = {
        ("dog", "barks"), ("dog", "barked"), ("cat", "meows"), ("cat", "meowed"),
        ("bird", "flies"), ("bird", "flew"), ("bird", "sings"), ("bird", "sang"),
        ("fish", "swims"), ("fish", "swam"), ("baby", "cries"), ("baby", "cried"),
        ("teacher", "teaches"), ("teacher", "taught"), ("chef", "cooks"),
        ("chef", "cooked"), ("driver", "drives"), ("driver", "drove"),
        ("singer", "sings"), ("singer", "sang"), ("artist", "paints"),
        ("artist", "painted"), ("writer", "writes"), ("writer", "wrote"),
        ("doctor", "heals"), ("doctor", "healed"), ("dancer", "dances"),
        ("dancer", "danced"), ("horse", "runs"), ("horse", "ran"),
        ("lion", "roars"), ("lion", "roared"), ("clock", "ticks"),
        ("clock", "ticked"), ("wind", "blows"), ("wind", "blew"),
        ("rain", "falls"), ("rain", "fell"), ("sun", "shines"),
        ("sun", "shone"), ("river", "flows"), ("river", "flowed"),
    }

    # Identify subject (skip articles) and verb
    articles = {"the", "a", "an"}
    tokens = [w for w in words]
    subject = None
    verb = None
    idx = 0
    while idx < len(tokens) and tokens[idx] in articles:
        idx += 1
    if idx < len(tokens):
        subject = tokens[idx]
        idx += 1
    if idx < len(tokens):
        verb = tokens[idx]

    if subject is None or verb is None:
        return False

    # Condition 1: subject and verb do NOT typically go together
    not_typical = (subject, verb) not in typical

    # Verb passive form (ends in -ed)
    passive = verb.endswith("ed")

    # Condition 2: prepositional phrase check
    has_special = ("by night" in t) or ("at dawn" in t) or ("near home" in t)

    if has_special and not passive:
        cond2 = False
    else:
        cond2 = True

    return not_typical and cond2

        "snake": {"slither", "slithers", "hiss", "hisses", "bite", "bites"},
        "frog": {"jump", "jumps", "hop", "hops", "croak", "croaks", "leap", "leaps"},
    }

    words = text.lower().replace(",", " ").replace(".", " ").split()
    if not words:
        return False

    # Find a known subject in the sentence.
    subj = None
    subj_idx = None
    for i, w in enumerate(words):
        key = w.strip("'s")
        if key in typical:
            subj = key
            subj_idx = i
            break

    if subj is None:
        return False

    # Find a verb after the subject from the known set or anywhere.
    all_verbs = set()
    for vs in typical.values():
        all_verbs |= vs

    found_verb = None
    for w in words[subj_idx + 1:]:
        if w in all_verbs:
            found_verb = w
            break
    if found_verb is None:
        # check whole sentence
        for w in words:
            if w in all_verbs:
                found_verb = w
                break
    if found_verb is None:
        return False

    # True if verb is NOT typical for this subject.
    return found_verb not in typical[subj]

        "pleasant", "wonderful", "great", "lovely", "delightful", "amazing",
        "excellent", "fantastic", "beautiful", "nice", "good", "enjoyable",
        "marvelous", "superb", "charming", "gorgeous", "splendid", "joyful",
        "happy", "fabulous", "terrific", "perfect", "brilliant", "magnificent",
    ]
    negative = [

        "unpleasant", "disgusting", "miserable", "lousy", "atrocious",
        "appalling", "abysmal", "horrendous", "ugly", "gross", "disappointing",
        "sad", "boring", "painful", "worst",
    ]

    pos = sum(lower.count(w) for w in positive)
    neg = sum(lower.count(w) for w in negative)

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # Extract the first word (the subject)
    words = text.strip().split()
    if not words:
        return False
    
    subject = words[0]
    # Strip trailing punctuation from the subject word
    subject_clean = subject.strip(".,!?;:'\"")
    
    if not subject_clean:
        return False
    
    # Singular indefinite pronouns
    singular_indefinite = {
        "Someone", "Somebody", "Something",
        "Anyone", "Anybody", "Anything",
        "Everyone", "Everybody", "Everything",
        "No one", "Nobody", "Nothing",
        "Each", "Either", "Neither",
        "One", "Whoever", "Whatever",
    }
    
    if subject_clean in singular_indefinite:
        return True
    
    # Check for "No one" as two words
    if len(words) >= 2:
        two_word = subject_clean + " " + words[1].strip(".,!?;:'\"")
        if two_word in singular_indefinite:
            return True
    
    # Singular proper noun: capitalized, alphabetic,
    # and not a plural (doesn't end in 's' in a plural-like way)
    if subject_clean[0].isupper() and subject_clean.isalpha():
        # Exclude common pronouns / non-proper-noun capitalized words
        common_words = {
            "The", "A", "An", "This", "That", "These", "Those",
            "He", "She", "It", "They", "We", "You", "I",
            "Who", "What", "Which", "When", "Where", "Why", "How",
            "His", "Her", "Its", "Their", "Our", "Your", "My",
            "All", "Some", "Many", "Few", "Several", "Both",
            "And", "But", "Or", "If", "Because", "While",
            "There", "Here",
        }
        if subject_clean in common_words:
            return False
        
        # Heuristic for plural proper nouns ending in 's'
        # (e.g., "Smiths") - treat as plural, return False
        if subject_clean.endswith("s") and not subject_clean.endswith("ss"):
            # Could be plural proper noun
            return False
        
        return True
    
    return False

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    singular_indefinite_pronouns = {
        "anybody", "anyone", "anything",
        "everybody", "everyone", "everything",
        "nobody", "no one", "nothing",
        "somebody", "someone", "something",
        "each", "either", "neither", "one",
        "another", "much",
    }

    s = text.strip()
    if not s:
        return False

    # Get the first "word token" (the subject candidate)
    # Handle multi-word indefinite pronouns like "no one"
    lowered = s.lower()
    for pron in ("no one", "no-one"):
        if lowered == pron or lowered.startswith(pron + " "):
            return True

    # Split into tokens
    tokens = s.split()
    if not tokens:
        return False

    first = tokens[0]

    # Strip leading/trailing punctuation from first token
    cleaned = first.strip(".,;:!?\"'()[]{}")
    if not cleaned:
        return False

    # Check indefinite pronoun
    if cleaned.lower() in singular_indefinite_pronouns:
        return True

    # Check singular proper noun: capitalized, not a sentence-start common word
    # A proper noun starts with a capital letter and rest are not all caps acronym requirement.
    if cleaned[0].isupper() and cleaned[1:].islower() or (cleaned[0].isupper() and len(cleaned) == 1):
        # Exclude plural proper nouns (ending in 's' suggesting plural)
        # Singular proper noun: treat as True
        if cleaned.endswith("s") and not cleaned.endswith("ss"):
            return False
        return True

    return False

        "freedom", "liberty", "equality", "democracy", "patience"
    }

    if lower in occupations:
        return False
    if lower in abstracts:
        return False

    if is_always:
        return True

    # Detect plural noun heuristically
    if len(lower) < 3:
        return False

    # Exclude words that look like adverbs/adjectives
    if lower.endswith("ly") or lower.endswith("ous") or lower.endswith("ive"):
        return False

    # Plural detection
    if lower.endswith("ss"):
        return False  # e.g., "grass", "class"
    if lower.endswith("ies"):
        return True
    if lower.endswith("es"):
        return True
    if lower.endswith("s") and not lower.endswith("us"):
        return True

    return False

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    words = text.strip().split()
    if len(words) < 2:
        return False

    # Lowercase tokens
    tokens = [w.strip(".,!?;:\"'").lower() for w in words]

    # Determiners that may precede subject
    determiners = {"a", "an", "the", "this", "that", "these", "those",
                   "some", "many", "two", "three", "several", "my", "your",
                   "his", "her", "its", "our", "their"}

    # Find subject (first non-determiner noun-like word)
    idx = 0
    while idx < len(tokens) and tokens[idx] in determiners:
        idx += 1
    if idx >= len(tokens):
        return False
    subject = tokens[idx]

    # Professions -> False
    professions = {"doctor", "teacher", "lawyer", "engineer", "nurse",
                   "driver", "pilot", "chef", "artist", "writer", "singer",
                   "actor", "actress", "scientist", "manager", "officer",
                   "clerk", "farmer", "baker", "soldier", "judge", "dentist",
                   "plumber", "painter", "waiter", "waitress", "professor",
                   "student", "worker", "player", "doctors", "teachers"}
    if subject in professions:
        return False

    # Numbers / adverbs -> False
    numbers = {"one", "two", "three", "four", "five", "six", "seven",
               "eight", "nine", "ten", "zero", "hundred", "thousand"}
    if subject in numbers or subject.isdigit():
        return False
    if subject.endswith("ly"):
        return False

    # Concrete countable nouns (singular set)
    concrete = {
        "dog", "cat", "bird", "fish", "horse", "cow", "sheep", "pig",
        "lion", "tiger", "bear", "rabbit", "mouse", "snake", "frog",
        "ball", "book", "chair", "table", "cup", "box", "car", "truck",
        "bike", "tree", "flower", "rock", "stone", "leaf", "stick",
        "house", "door", "window", "wall", "roof", "boat", "ship",
        "plane", "train", "phone", "computer", "pen", "pencil", "knife",
        "spoon", "fork", "plate", "bottle", "glass", "lamp", "clock",
        "bag", "shoe", "hat", "coat", "shirt", "child", "baby", "man",
        "woman", "boy", "girl", "apple", "banana", "orange", "egg",
        "bread", "stick", "wheel", "engine", "machine", "robot", "toy",
        "ant", "bee", "butterfly", "duck", "chicken", "elephant",
        "monkey", "deer", "wolf", "fox", "spider", "worm", "bug"
    }

    # Build plural forms automatically
    def pluralize(n):
        forms = {n + "s"}
        if n.endswith("y"):
            forms.add(n[:-1] + "ies")
        if n.endswith(("s", "x", "z", "ch", "sh")):
            forms.add(n + "es")
        return forms

    plural_to_singular = {}
    for n in concrete:
        for pl in pluralize(n):
            plural_to_singular[pl] = n

    irregular = {"children": "child", "men": "man", "women": "woman",
                 "mice": "mouse", "feet": "foot", "teeth": "tooth",
                 "geese": "goose", "people": "person", "leaves": "leaf",
                 "knives": "knife", "lives": "life", "wolves": "wolf"}
    plural_to_singular.update(irregular)

    is_concrete = False
    base = None
    if subject in concrete:
        is_concrete = True
        base = subject
    elif subject in plural_to_singular:
        is_concrete = True
        base = plural_to_singular[subject]

    if not is_concrete:
        return False

    # Find verb after subject
    rest = tokens[idx + 1:]
    if not rest:
        return False

    # Plausible physical action/state verbs (common)
    physical_verbs = {
        "runs", "run", "walks", "walk", "jumps", "jump", "sits", "sit",
        "stands", "stand", "lies", "lie", "sleeps", "sleep", "eats", "eat",
        "drinks", "drink", "moves", "move", "falls", "fall", "flies", "fly",
        "swims", "swim", "rolls", "roll", "spins", "spin", "breaks", "break",
        "bounces", "bounce", "floats", "float", "sinks", "sink", "rests",
        "rest", "grows", "grow", "shines", "shine", "glows", "glow", "burns",
        "burn", "barks", "bark", "meows", "meow", "sings", "sing", "crawls",
        "crawl", "climbs", "climb", "rotates", "rotate", "drops", "drop",
        "slides", "slide", "shakes", "shake", "opens", "open", "closes",
        "close", "turns", "turn", "stops", "stop", "starts", "start",
        "hangs", "hang", "floats", "is", "are", "was", "were", "stays",
        "stay", "waits", "wait", "lands", "land", "rises", "rise",
        "plays", "play", "hops", "hop", "wags", "wag", "purrs", "purr",
        "flaps", "flap", "ticks", "tick", "rings", "ring", "breaks"
    }

    verb = rest[0]
    if verb in physical_verbs:
        return True

    return False

articulation: The label is True if the subject is a plural noun or adverb that is not a flower, river, or number, and is not "Firmly," otherwise the label is False.
code:
def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    word = text.strip()

    # Exclude empty
    if not word:
        return False

    # Normalize for comparisons
    core = word.rstrip(".,!?;:").strip()
    lower = core.lower()

    # Exclude "Firmly" specifically
    if core == "Firmly" or lower == "firmly":
        return False

    # Exclude numbers (digit-based or number words)
    if lower.replace(".", "").replace(",", "").isdigit():
        return False
    number_words = {
        "zero", "one", "two", "three", "four", "five", "six", "seven",
        "eight", "nine", "ten", "eleven", "twelve", "thirteen", "fourteen",
        "fifteen", "sixteen", "seventeen", "eighteen", "nineteen", "twenty",
        "thirty", "forty", "fifty", "sixty", "seventy", "eighty", "ninety",
        "hundred", "thousand", "million", "billion",
        "first", "second", "third", "fourth", "fifth", "sixth", "seventh",
        "eighth", "ninth", "tenth",
    }
    if lower in number_words:
        return False

    # Exclude flowers
    flowers = {
        "rose", "roses", "tulip", "tulips", "daisy", "daisies", "lily",
        "lilies", "orchid", "orchids", "sunflower", "sunflowers", "daffodil",
        "daffodils", "marigold", "marigolds", "carnation", "carnations",
        "petunia", "petunias", "lavender", "iris", "irises", "violet",
        "violets", "poppy", "poppies", "lotus", "jasmine", "begonia",
        "begonias", "geranium", "geraniums", "hyacinth", "hyacinths",
        "chrysanthemum", "chrysanthemums", "dandelion", "dandelions",
        "peony", "peonies", "azalea", "azaleas", "magnolia", "magnolias",
    }
    if lower in flowers:
        return False

    # Exclude rivers
    rivers = {
        "nile", "amazon", "yangtze", "mississippi", "missouri", "ganges",
        "danube", "thames", "rhine", "volga", "seine", "tigris",
        "euphrates", "mekong", "congo", "niger", "zambezi", "yukon",
        "rivers", "river",
    }
    if lower in rivers:
        return False

    # Detect adverb (commonly ends in -ly)
    is_adverb = lower.endswith("ly") and len(lower) > 2

    # Detect plural noun (heuristic: ends in 's' but not 'ss', not single letter)
    is_plural = (
        lower.endswith("s")
        and not lower.endswith("ss")
        and len(lower) > 2
        and not lower.endswith("us")
    )

    return is_adverb or is_plural

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # Heuristic implementation:
    # True iff subject and first object are both inanimate nouns
    # and the main verb is a plausible action.

    animate_words = {
        "i", "you", "he", "she", "we", "they", "me", "him", "her", "us", "them",
        "man", "woman", "men", "women", "boy", "girl", "child", "children",
        "person", "people", "dog", "cat", "bird", "horse", "cow", "fish",
        "teacher", "student", "doctor", "worker", "king", "queen", "baby",
        "mother", "father", "sister", "brother", "friend", "animal", "lion",
        "mouse", "bear", "wolf", "snake", "monkey", "rabbit", "chef", "cook",
        "driver", "player", "soldier", "officer", "nurse", "farmer", "guy",
        "lady", "kid", "kids", "guys", "everyone", "someone", "somebody",
    }

    inanimate_words = {
        "rock", "stone", "table", "chair", "book", "ball", "box", "door",
        "window", "car", "truck", "wall", "tree", "house", "wheel", "engine",
        "machine", "computer", "phone", "cup", "plate", "bottle", "knife",
        "fork", "spoon", "bowl", "pan", "pot", "bread", "cake", "apple",
        "ladder", "rope", "wire", "lamp", "light", "fire", "water", "ice",
        "metal", "wood", "glass", "paper", "pen", "pencil", "key", "lock",
        "bridge", "road", "fence", "gate", "brick", "board", "hammer", "nail",
        "screw", "bolt", "gear", "lever", "button", "switch", "valve", "pipe",
        "tank", "barrel", "crate", "basket", "bag", "sack", "stick", "log",
        "block", "cube", "sphere", "plank", "beam", "rail", "track", "cart",
        "wagon", "boat", "ship", "plane", "rocket", "bomb", "bullet", "arrow",
        "spear", "sword", "shield", "armor", "helmet", "boot", "shoe", "hat",
        "coat", "shirt", "robot", "device", "tool", "object", "item", "thing",
    }

    action_verbs = {
        "push", "pushes", "pushed", "pull", "pulls", "pulled", "hit", "hits",
        "break", "breaks", "broke", "broken", "crush", "crushes", "crushed",
        "smash", "smashes", "smashed", "move", "moves", "moved", "roll",
        "rolls", "rolled", "slide", "slides", "slid", "drop", "drops",
        "dropped", "throw", "throws", "threw", "lift", "lifts", "lifted",
        "carry", "carries", "carried", "block", "blocks", "blocked", "cover",
        "covers", "covered", "support", "supports", "supported", "hold",
        "holds", "held", "strike", "strikes", "struck", "press", "presses",
        "pressed", "scratch", "scratches", "scratched", "cut", "cuts",
        "dent", "dents", "dented", "bend", "bends", "bent", "knock", "knocks",
        "knocked", "topple", "topples", "toppled", "shatter", "shatters",
        "shattered", "squash", "squashes", "squashed", "drag", "drags",
        "dragged", "support", "fill", "fills", "filled", "open", "opens",
        "opened", "close", "closes", "closed",
    }

    words = [w.strip(".,!?;:\"'()").lower() for w in text.split()]
    words = [w for w in words if w]

    # Find subject: first noun (skip leading articles/adjectives)
    skip = {"the", "a", "an", "this", "that", "these", "those", "his",
            "her", "its", "their", "my", "your", "some", "any", "big",
            "small", "old", "new", "heavy", "light"}

    def classify(w):
        if w in inanimate_words:
            return "inanimate"
        if w in animate_words:
            return "animate"
        return None

    # locate verb
    verb_idx = None
    for i, w in enumerate(words):
        if w in action_verbs:
            verb_idx = i
            break
    if verb_idx is None:
        return False

    # subject: a noun before the verb
    subject = None
    for i in range(verb_idx):
        c = classify(words[i])
        if c is not None:
            subject = c
            break

    # object: a noun after the verb
    obj = None
    for i in range(verb_idx + 1, len(words)):
        c = classify(words[i])
        if c is not None:
            obj = c
            break

    if subject is None or obj is None:
        return False

    return subject == "inanimate" and obj == "inanimate"

    # find first noun
    first_noun = None
    noun_index = None
    for i, w in enumerate(words):
        if w in inanimate_nouns or w in animate_nouns:
            first_noun = w
            noun_index = i

    if first_noun is None:

        if w in inanimate_verbs:
            return True
        if w in animate_nouns or w in inanimate_nouns:
            continue

    return False

    words = [w.strip(".,!?;:\"'()").lower() for w in text.split()]
    words = [w for w in words if w]

    subject = None

    for i, w in enumerate(words):
        if w in animate or w in inanimate:
            subject = w
            subj_idx = i
            break

    if subject is None:
        return False
    if subject in animate:

    return False

    idx = 0
    determiners = {"the", "a", "an", "this", "that", "these", "those", "my", "your", "his", "her", "its", "our", "their"}
    while idx < len(words) and words[idx].strip(".,!?;:'\"").lower() in determiners:
        idx += 1

    if idx >= len(words):
        return False

    subject = words[idx].strip(".,!?;:'\"").lower()
    if subject.endswith("s") and subject[:-1] in inanimate_subjects:
        subj_inanimate = True
    else:
        subj_inanimate = subject in inanimate_subjects

    if not subj_inanimate:
        return False

    # find verb: next token after subject
    for j in range(idx + 1, len(words)):
        candidate = words[j].strip(".,!?;:'\"").lower()
        if not candidate:
            continue
        if candidate in human_verbs:
            return False
        # assume first non-empty word after subject is the verb
        return True

    return False

    inanimate_subjects = {

        "branch", "leaf", "wave", "storm", "door", "wheel", "engine",

    text_stripped = text.strip()

    if len(words) < 2:

    # find subject: skip articles
    idx = 0
    while idx < len(words) and words[idx] in {"the", "a", "an", "this", "that", "these", "those", "my", "his", "her", "its", "their"}:
        idx += 1
    if idx >= len(words):

    subject = words[idx]
    is_inanimate = subject in inanimate_subjects

    if not (is_inanimate or is_body):

    return False

articulation: The label is True if and only if the subject has at least two adjectives and the verb is not "watched," "painted," "carried," "lifted," "closed," "sorted," "checked," or "packed," otherwise the label is False.
code:
def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    forbidden_verbs = {"watched", "painted", "carried", "lifted",
                       "closed", "sorted", "checked", "packed"}

    words = text.strip().split()
    if not words:
        return False

    # Strip punctuation from tokens
    def clean(w):
        return w.strip(".,!?;:\"'()").lower()

    tokens = [clean(w) for w in words]
    tokens = [t for t in tokens if t]

    if not tokens:
        return False

    # Find the verb: first word that is a forbidden verb or ends in common verb forms
    # Heuristic: subject is the noun phrase before the verb.
    # Determiners
    determiners = {"the", "a", "an", "this", "that", "these", "those",
                   "my", "your", "his", "her", "its", "our", "their"}

    # Find verb index: look for a known verb or a word ending in 'ed' or 's'
    verb_index = None
    for i, t in enumerate(tokens):
        if t in forbidden_verbs:
            verb_index = i
            break

    # If no forbidden verb found, find the verb as the first verb-like token
    # after the subject (a word ending in 'ed', 's', or 'ing' past a noun)
    if verb_index is None:
        # find subject noun phrase start
        start = 0
        if tokens[0] in determiners:
            start = 1
        # subject = adjectives + noun; verb comes after the noun
        # locate the noun: scan from start, the verb is first token that looks like a verb
        for i in range(start, len(tokens)):
            t = tokens[i]
            if i > start and (t.endswith("ed") or t.endswith("ing") or
                              (t.endswith("s") and not t.endswith("ss"))):
                verb_index = i
                break
        if verb_index is None:
            verb_index = len(tokens)

    verb = tokens[verb_index] if verb_index < len(tokens) else ""

    # Subject tokens are before the verb
    subject_tokens = tokens[:verb_index]

    # Remove leading determiner
    if subject_tokens and subject_tokens[0] in determiners:
        subject_tokens = subject_tokens[1:]

    # The last token is the noun; preceding tokens are adjectives
    if len(subject_tokens) < 1:
        adjectives = []
    else:
        adjectives = subject_tokens[:-1]

    has_two_adj = len(adjectives) >= 2
    verb_ok = verb not in forbidden_verbs

    return has_two_adj and verb_ok

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # Heuristic approach without external libraries.
    # We look for a verb, then a direct object (noun phrase),
    # and check whether that noun is preceded by at least two adjectives.

    words = text.replace(",", " ").split()
    if not words:
        return False

    # Small dictionaries of common words for a heuristic check.
    common_verbs = {
        "ate", "saw", "found", "bought", "made", "had", "held", "wore",
        "carried", "took", "grabbed", "picked", "ordered", "wanted",
        "needed", "used", "drove", "kicked", "threw", "caught", "built",
        "painted", "drew", "read", "wrote", "cooked", "baked", "chose",
        "liked", "loved", "hated", "owned", "kept", "lost", "broke",
        "opened", "closed", "cleaned", "cleaned", "washed", "examined",
        "noticed", "spotted", "described", "gave", "brought", "sold",
        "ate", "drank", "preferred", "selected", "purchased", "got",
        "eats", "sees", "finds", "buys", "makes", "wears", "wants",
        "needs", "holds", "carries", "takes", "grabs", "picks",
    }

    determiners = {"a", "an", "the", "this", "that", "these", "those",
                   "his", "her", "their", "my", "your", "our", "some",
                   "many", "few", "several"}

    # Adjective heuristic: not a verb, not determiner, not obviously a noun-only,
    # use common adjective set plus -ish/-ful/-ous/-y suffix guesses.
    common_adjectives = {
        "big", "small", "little", "large", "huge", "tiny", "old", "new",
        "young", "good", "bad", "great", "nice", "fine", "ugly", "pretty",
        "beautiful", "red", "green", "blue", "yellow", "black", "white",
        "brown", "gray", "grey", "orange", "purple", "pink", "soft", "hard",
        "warm", "cold", "hot", "cool", "fast", "slow", "long", "short",
        "tall", "round", "square", "heavy", "light", "dark", "bright",
        "clean", "dirty", "fresh", "stale", "sweet", "sour", "bitter",
        "salty", "spicy", "rich", "poor", "happy", "sad", "angry", "calm",
        "quiet", "loud", "smooth", "rough", "sharp", "dull", "wet", "dry",
        "thick", "thin", "wide", "narrow", "deep", "shallow", "strong",
        "weak", "expensive", "cheap", "shiny", "rusty", "wooden", "metal",
        "plastic", "golden", "silver", "delicious", "tasty", "fragrant",
        "fluffy", "furry", "scary", "funny", "strange", "odd", "weird",
        "lovely", "gorgeous", "elegant", "modern", "ancient", "antique",
        "tired", "hungry", "thirsty", "lazy", "busy", "empty", "full",
    }

    def is_adjective(w):
        lw = w.lower()
        if lw in common_adjectives:
            return True
        if lw in determiners or lw in common_verbs:
            return False
        # suffix-based guess
        for suf in ("ful", "ous", "ish", "less", "able", "ible", "ive"):
            if lw.endswith(suf) and len(lw) > len(suf) + 2:
                return True
        return False

    n = len(words)
    for i, w in enumerate(words):
        if w.lower() in common_verbs:
            # find start of object phrase after the verb
            j = i + 1
            # skip a determiner
            if j < n and words[j].lower() in determiners:
                j += 1
            # count consecutive adjectives
            count = 0
            while j < n and is_adjective(words[j]):
                count += 1
                j += 1
            if count >= 2:
                return True
    return False

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    # Heuristic: detect if the object (typically after a verb/preposition or
    # as a noun phrase) is modified by at least two adjectives.
    # We approximate adjectives by a known list plus common suffixes.

    adj_suffixes = ("ful", "ous", "ive", "able", "ible", "less", "al",
                    "ish", "ent", "ant", "ary", "ic")
    common_adjs = {
        "big", "small", "large", "tiny", "huge", "little", "tall", "short",
        "long", "old", "new", "young", "good", "bad", "red", "blue", "green",
        "yellow", "black", "white", "brown", "gray", "grey", "orange",
        "purple", "pink", "hot", "cold", "warm", "cool", "fast", "slow",
        "hard", "soft", "heavy", "light", "dark", "bright", "clean", "dirty",
        "wet", "dry", "full", "empty", "open", "closed", "sharp", "dull",
        "round", "square", "flat", "deep", "shallow", "thick", "thin",
        "wide", "narrow", "rich", "poor", "happy", "sad", "angry", "calm",
        "quiet", "loud", "strong", "weak", "fat", "wide", "smooth", "rough",
        "fresh", "stale", "sweet", "sour", "bitter", "salty", "spicy",
        "shiny", "rusty", "broken", "fixed", "tired", "fancy", "plain",
        "ancient", "modern", "wooden", "metal", "golden", "silver", "tasty",
        "ugly", "pretty", "beautiful", "nice", "great", "wonderful",
        "terrible", "awful", "scary", "funny", "serious", "simple", "complex",
        "easy", "difficult", "expensive", "cheap", "rare", "common",
    }

    def is_adj(w):
        wl = w.lower().strip(".,!?;:\"'()")
        if not wl.isalpha():
            return False
        if wl in common_adjs:
            return True
        if len(wl) > 4 and wl.endswith(adj_suffixes):
            return True
        return False

    words = text.split()
    # Find the longest run of consecutive adjective-like words.
    max_run = 0
    run = 0
    for w in words:
        if is_adj(w):
            run += 1
            if run > max_run:
                max_run = run
        else:
            run = 0
    return max_run >= 2

    obj_adj = sum(1 for w in object_words if w in adjectives)

    adjectives = {

        "tame", "gentle", "fierce", "clever", "stupid", "lazy", "busy",
    }

    def split_words(s):
        word = ""
        words = []
        for ch in s:

                    words.append(word)
                    word = ""
        if word:

        "pull", "pulls", "pulled", "throw", "throws", "threw", "thrown",
    }

    words = split_words(text)
    lower = [w.lower() for w in words]

    verb_idx = None
    for i, w in enumerate(lower):
        if w in verbs:
            verb_idx = i
            break

    if verb_idx is None:
        subject_words = lower
        object_words = []
    else:
        subject_words = lower[:verb_idx]
        object_words = lower[verb_idx + 1:]

    subj_adj = sum(1 for w in subject_words if w in adjectives)
    obj_adj = sum(1 for w in object_words if w in adjectives)

    return subj_adj >= 2 or obj_adj >= 2

def rule(text: str) -> bool:
    words = text.replace(",", " ").replace(".", " ").split()
    words = [w.lower() for w in words if w.strip()]

    # Common adjective suffixes/words heuristic
    adj_suffixes = ("able", "ible", "ful", "ous", "ive", "ant", "ent",
                    "ary", "ic", "al", "ish", "less", "y")
    common_adjs = {"big", "small", "old", "new", "good", "bad", "great",
                   "long", "short", "high", "low", "hot", "cold", "fast",
                   "slow", "red", "blue", "green", "black", "white", "tall",
                   "wide", "deep", "rich", "poor", "young", "happy", "sad",
                   "hard", "easy", "soft", "loud", "quiet", "bright", "dark"}

    def is_adj(w):
        if w in common_adjs:
            return True
        if len(w) > 4 and w.endswith(adj_suffixes):
            return True
        return False

    def is_adv(w):
        return w.endswith("ly") and len(w) > 3

    # Count adjectives (proxy for subject having at least two)
    adj_count = sum(1 for w in words if is_adj(w))
    has_adv = any(is_adv(w) for w in words)

    return adj_count >= 2 or has_adv
